title: Board 2026 02 18
author: Sarah Chen
content:
MERIDIAN DYNAMICS, INC.
BOARD OF DIRECTORS MEETING MINUTES

Date: February 18, 2026
Time: 9:00 a.m. – 12:47 p.m. Central Time
Location: Meridian Dynamics Headquarters, 4400 Domain Boulevard, Suite 900, Austin, Texas 78758
(Remote participants connected via encrypted Zoom enterprise session)

Prepared by: Sarah Chen, Assistant Corporate Secretary
Classification: STRICTLY CONFIDENTIAL – BOARD AND AUTHORIZED COUNSEL ONLY

---

CALL TO ORDER

The meeting was called to order at 9:04 a.m. Central Time by David Hargrove, Chief Executive Officer and Chairman of the Board. Sarah Chen confirmed that notice of the meeting had been duly provided to all directors in accordance with the Company's bylaws and applicable Delaware General Corporation Law requirements.

---

ROLL CALL AND ESTABLISHMENT OF QUORUM

Directors present:
- David Hargrove, Chief Executive Officer and Chairman
- James Whitfield, Lead Independent Director
- Diana Santos, Independent Director
- Robert Kim, Independent Director

Management and officers present:
- Lisa Phan, Chief Technology Officer
- Raymond Okafor, Chief Financial Officer
- Margaret Liu, General Counsel

Also present:
- Sarah Chen, Assistant Corporate Secretary
- Peter Albright, Partner, Latham & Watkins LLP (outside counsel), participating via video
- Caroline Reyes, Managing Director, Goldman Sachs & Co. LLC (financial advisor), participating via video

Hargrove confirmed that all four directors were present and that a quorum was duly established. He noted that the agenda for today's meeting was distributed under separate cover on February 14, 2026, along with the supporting board materials packet, and that all materials had been transmitted via the Company's encrypted board portal.

---

APPROVAL OF AGENDA

Hargrove moved to adopt the agenda as distributed. Santos seconded. The agenda was adopted by unanimous voice vote.

---

APPROVAL OF PRIOR MEETING MINUTES

Hargrove asked whether there were any corrections or comments regarding the minutes of the January 14, 2026 regular board meeting. No corrections were offered. Whitfield moved to approve the minutes as presented. Kim seconded. The minutes were approved by unanimous voice vote.

---

ITEM 1: TAIPEI FABRICATION FACILITY UPDATE

Hargrove invited Phan to provide an update on the Taipei fab following the January thermal incident.

Phan reported that the thermal excursion event, which occurred on January 14, 2026 in Bay 5 of the Taipei facility, had been contained without injury to personnel and without loss of product currently in active production runs. She stated that the root cause analysis, completed February 9 by the Taipei facility engineering team in conjunction with the equipment OEM, identified a failure in a secondary coolant bypass valve that had not been flagged during the most recent preventive maintenance cycle conducted in November 2025. Phan confirmed that all analogous valves across both Taipei and Dresden had been inspected and cleared as of February 12.

Phan noted that the incident resulted in approximately eleven days of reduced throughput in Axis chiplet assembly, with an estimated production impact of roughly $3.1 million in delayed revenue, which Okafor confirmed would be reflected in Q1 2026 results. Insurance recovery proceedings have been initiated; Liu confirmed that the applicable policy limit for equipment-related business interruption is $5 million per occurrence.

Whitfield asked whether the maintenance protocol failure represented a systemic gap or an isolated lapse. Phan responded that the engineering team's assessment pointed to a documentation gap in the valve classification system used during PM scheduling, and that a revised classification framework would be implemented across both fabs by March 31, 2026.

Kim asked whether the Dresden facility had been audited under the same revised protocol. Phan confirmed it had and that Dresden was clear.

No action items were generated beyond the existing internal engineering remediation plan. The Board acknowledged the update.

---

ITEM 2: Q4 AND FULL YEAR 2025 LIGHTHOUSE PROGRAM REVIEW

Hargrove invited Phan to present the Q4 Lighthouse progress review, noting the full written report had been included in the board materials packet.

Phan summarized the Lighthouse program's Q4 2025 results. The Lumen Gen 2 training accelerator achieved its target performance threshold of 4.8 petaflops at BF16 precision in internal validation on December 19, 2025, two weeks ahead of the revised schedule. She noted that tape-out for the production mask set was completed January 31, 2026, and that first silicon from TSMC is expected in late April 2026. The Pilot edge inference line achieved volume production qualification at the Dresden fab in November 2025, with 94,000 units shipped to three OEM customers in Q4.

Santos asked about design win momentum for Lumen Gen 2. Phan indicated that the sales team, working with three hyperscaler prospects under NDA, had advanced two of the three to formal evaluation agreements as of January 2026, with a third engagement paused pending procurement cycle timing on the customer side. Hargrove added that one of those engagements was at a stage where pricing and volume commitments were under active negotiation.

The Board acknowledged the Q4 Lighthouse review. No formal vote was required.

---

ITEM 3: PROPOSED ACQUISITION BY APEX INDUSTRIES – STRATEGIC REVIEW AND BOARD AUTHORIZATION

Hargrove reminded all participants that this item and all information discussed under it are subject to the Company's existing non-disclosure protocols and that, per the Board's prior direction, knowledge of this matter remains restricted to individuals present at this meeting. He asked Albright and Reyes to confirm their continued obligations under the applicable NDAs, which both did on the record.

Hargrove provided a brief chronological summary. He stated that Apex Industries first approached Meridian in October 2025 through informal discussions between himself and Apex's CEO, followed by a formal written expression of interest received November 14, 2025. Goldman Sachs was engaged as Meridian's exclusive financial advisor in November 2025, and Latham & Watkins was engaged as outside M&A counsel. A formal negotiation process has been conducted over the past three months.

Reyes presented Goldman Sachs's updated financial analysis. She stated that the proposed transaction values Meridian at $4.2 billion, representing a per-share price of $38.00, payable in a combination of cash and Apex common stock at a ratio to be elected by Meridian shareholders subject to proration provisions. She noted that the $38.00 per share figure represents a premium of approximately 34% over Meridian's 90-day volume-weighted average share price as of February 13, 2026. Reyes stated that Goldman Sachs had reviewed selected public company comparables, precedent transactions in the semiconductor and AI hardware sectors, and a discounted cash flow analysis, and that Goldman Sachs was prepared to deliver a fairness opinion to the Board in connection with execution of the definitive merger agreement.

Albright outlined the key terms of the proposed merger agreement as currently negotiated, including a go-shop period of 30 days following signing, a termination fee of $126 million payable by Meridian in certain circumstances, and a reverse termination fee of $210 million payable by Apex in the event of a regulatory failure to close. He noted that the transaction requires regulatory clearance in the United States under the HSR Act, in the European Union under the EU Merger Regulation, and in Taiwan under the Fair Trade Act, and that the parties' advisors estimate a regulatory review timeline of approximately five to seven months. Closing is targeted for September 2026.

Whitfield stated that he had reviewed the materials carefully and had several concerns he wished to place on the record. He expressed the view that the 30-day go-shop period was insufficient given the complexity of Meridian's technology portfolio and the limited universe of potential strategic acquirers who could credibly move in that timeframe. He stated that he would have preferred a 45-day go-shop and asked Albright whether there was any remaining room to negotiate that point. Albright responded that Apex had been firm on 30 days and that pushing further risked reopening other negotiated terms. Whitfield indicated he was not satisfied with that answer and reserved the right to vote against the final agreement if the go-shop window was not extended.

Santos asked about employee protections post-close. Albright stated that the merger agreement includes a covenant requiring Apex to maintain substantially equivalent compensation and benefits for Meridian employees for a period of twelve months following closing, though he acknowledged that covenant does not constrain Apex's longer-term organizational decisions.

Kim asked Reyes whether Goldman Sachs had stress-tested the DCF against downside scenarios reflecting potential delays in Lumen Gen 2 commercial ramp. Reyes confirmed that the analysis included a downside case reflecting a six-month ramp delay and that the $38.00 per share consideration remained above the midpoint of the DCF range in that scenario.

Following discussion, Hargrove asked for a motion to authorize management to proceed to execution of the definitive merger agreement in substantially the form reviewed by the Board, subject to final legal review by Latham & Watkins and delivery of the Goldman Sachs fairness opinion.

Liu moved to authorize execution. Santos seconded.

Vote:
- Hargrove: In favor
- Santos: In favor
- Kim: In favor
- Whitfield: Opposed (stated for the record: "I support the transaction strategically but cannot vote in favor until the go-shop provision is resolved to my satisfaction")

Motion carried, three votes in favor, one opposed.

Albright confirmed he would communicate the Board's position on the go-shop to Apex's counsel and report back no later than February 23, 2026.

---

ITEM 4: WORKFORCE RESTRUCTURING PLAN – APPROVAL

Hargrove introduced this item and invited Okafor to present.

Okafor stated that management had completed a comprehensive review of the Company's cost structure in light of anticipated integration requirements and current market conditions. He recommended a workforce reduction of approximately 7% of global headcount, representing approximately 290 positions, concentrated in corporate functions and the global sales organization. He stated that R&D headcount, including engineering teams supporting Lighthouse program deliverables, would not be affected. Okafor noted that the annualized run-rate savings are estimated at $31 million.

Okafor stated that the severance terms proposed include 12 weeks of base salary continuation plus healthcare benefit continuation through the end of the severance period, for all affected employees regardless of tenure. He estimated total one-time severance and related costs at approximately $8.4 million, to be recognized in Q3 2026.

Hargrove stated that the announcement is planned for early Q3 2026, coordinated with the public announcement of the Apex transaction and in a manner consistent with applicable WARN Act obligations and international labor notification requirements in Germany.

Santos asked whether the 12-week package was benchmarked against peer practice. Okafor confirmed that the package was reviewed against comparable restructuring actions at peer semiconductor companies over the past 24 months and was at or above median.

Whitfield moved to approve the workforce restructuring plan as presented. Kim seconded.

Vote: Unanimous in favor.

---

ACTION ITEMS SUMMARY

| # | Action Item | Owner | Due Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Implement revised coolant valve classification framework across Taipei and Dresden fabs | Lisa Phan | March 31, 2026 |
| 2 | Communicate Board's go-shop position to Apex counsel; report outcome to Board | Peter Albright | February 23, 2026 |
| 3 | Deliver Goldman Sachs fairness opinion in connection with merger agreement signing | Caroline Reyes / Goldman Sachs | TBD (prior to execution) |
| 4 | Finalize definitive merger agreement for execution subject to Board authorization | Margaret Liu / Peter Albright | TBD |
| 5 | Prepare WARN Act and German notification compliance schedule for workforce reduction | Margaret Liu | March 15, 2026 |
| 6 | Prepare employee communication plan for Q3 workforce reduction announcement | Raymond Okafor | April 30, 2026 |

---

ADJOURNMENT

There being no further business, Hargrove declared the meeting adjourned at 12:47 p.m. Central Time.

---

Respectfully submitted,

Sarah Chen
Assistant Corporate Secretary
Meridian Dynamics, Inc.

Approved by the Board of Directors: ___________________________
Date: ___________________________

---

CONFIDENTIALITY NOTICE: These minutes contain material non-public information subject to securities laws and the Company's insider trading policy. Distribution is strictly prohibited outside of authorized recipients. Any unauthorized disclosure may result in civil and criminal liability.

title: Board 2025 12 10
author: Sarah Chen
content:
MERIDIAN DYNAMICS, INC.
BOARD OF DIRECTORS MEETING — MINUTES (DRAFT)
December 10, 2025
CONFIDENTIAL — PRIVILEGED AND SUBJECT TO ATTORNEY-CLIENT PRIVILEGE

Meeting convened at 9:04 a.m. Central Time at the company's principal offices, 4400 Domain Boulevard, Suite 900, Austin, Texas 78758. The meeting was held in person with no remote attendees.

ATTENDEES

Directors Present:
James Whitfield, Lead Independent Director (Chair, Audit Committee)
Diana Santos, Director
Robert Kim, Director

Management Present:
David Hargrove, Chief Executive Officer
Lisa Phan, Chief Technology Officer
Raymond Okafor, Chief Financial Officer
Margaret Liu, General Counsel

Secretary:
Sarah Chen, Assistant Corporate Secretary

A quorum of the Board of Directors of Meridian Dynamics, Inc. (the "Company") was present.

---

CALL TO ORDER AND PRELIMINARY MATTERS

Mr. Whitfield called the meeting to order and confirmed a quorum. Mr. Hargrove welcomed the attendees and noted that the agenda for this meeting included three items requiring the Board's full attention: a strategic matter of significant sensitivity, a technology program update, and an executive session. He requested that the Board hold questions on the strategic matter until he had completed his full presentation. Ms. Liu distributed a one-page confidentiality acknowledgment to all attendees, which each person signed prior to further discussion. Ms. Liu stated for the record that all matters discussed in the meeting were subject to strict embargo and were not to be disclosed outside the individuals present.

---

ITEM 1: STRATEGIC UPDATE — UNSOLICITED APPROACH FROM APEX INDUSTRIES

Mr. Hargrove addressed the Board and stated that the purpose of this agenda item was to formally brief the full Board on a development that he, Mr. Okafor, and Ms. Liu had been managing on a preliminary basis since late October. He acknowledged that the timing of this briefing had been deliberate, as the Company's advisors had counseled that the approach be assessed for seriousness before the Board was convened, and that in his judgment and the judgment of counsel that threshold had now been reached.

Mr. Hargrove described the sequence of events as follows. In mid-October 2025, he received a direct communication from Gerald Farr, Chief Executive Officer of Apex Industries, Inc., a diversified technology conglomerate headquartered in San Jose with approximately $28 billion in annual revenue and operations spanning enterprise infrastructure, industrial automation, and semiconductor distribution. The preliminary Apex approach from October was framed as an exploratory conversation, with Mr. Farr indicating that Apex had been studying Meridian's position in AI inference silicon and believed there was a compelling strategic rationale for combination. Mr. Hargrove stated that he responded noncommittally, informed Mr. Okafor and Ms. Liu immediately thereafter, and engaged Goldman Sachs as financial advisor to the Company within the following week.

Mr. Hargrove then outlined the current state of the proposal. Apex has communicated a proposed acquisition price of $38.00 per share in a mix of cash and Apex common stock, implying a total transaction value of approximately $4.2 billion based on the Company's diluted share count. The cash-to-stock split has not been finalized. Apex has indicated it is prepared to move expeditiously toward a definitive agreement and has retained Morgan Stanley as its financial advisor. Regulatory approval would be required in the United States, the European Union, and Taiwan, given the Company's fabrication relationships in Taipei and Dresden, with a targeted transaction close in the third quarter of 2026.

Mr. Hargrove paused and opened the floor to initial reactions.

Mr. Whitfield asked whether the $38.00 figure represented Apex's opening position or a firm offer. Mr. Hargrove indicated that Goldman Sachs had characterized it as a serious opening indication rather than a take-it-or-leave-it number, and that the advisors believed there was room to negotiate on both price and structure. Mr. Whitfield noted that the 30-day average closing price for MRDC shares as of the prior Friday was approximately $28.40, implying a premium of roughly 34 percent, and asked Mr. Okafor whether Goldman had offered a preliminary view on adequacy. Mr. Okafor stated that Goldman had not yet delivered a formal opinion, as the engagement was recent and the parties had not yet exchanged diligence materials, but that the bank's preliminary reaction was that the premium was within the range seen in comparable semiconductor transactions over the past three years, though on the lower end when accounting for the Company's pipeline.

Ms. Santos asked what Apex's stated strategic rationale was. Mr. Hargrove said Apex had expressed interest in vertically integrating inference silicon capability into its enterprise infrastructure business and viewed Meridian's Axis product line and its relationships with hyperscale customers as the primary assets. He added that Apex had also referenced the Company's edge inference business and expressed interest in the Pilot roadmap, though he characterized that as secondary.

Mr. Kim asked directly about Project Lighthouse and whether Apex was aware of it. Mr. Hargrove said Apex had not been given any information about Lighthouse, and that the program had not been referenced in any communications with Apex or Morgan Stanley. He stated that the Board would need to consider carefully how and when to handle Lighthouse in the context of any diligence process, and that this was one of the reasons Goldman and Ms. Liu had advised proceeding thoughtfully.

Ms. Liu noted that the Board would need to form a special committee of independent directors to oversee any transaction process, and that she would work with outside M&A counsel to structure that committee appropriately. She recommended that the Board authorize the formation of such a committee at this meeting or at a reconvened meeting in January, depending on how quickly Apex indicated it wished to proceed. Mr. Whitfield said he was prepared to serve on the committee and noted that Ms. Santos and Mr. Kim should discuss among themselves their availability and any potential conflicts. Both directors confirmed they saw no conflicts at this time and expressed willingness to serve.

The Board discussed process and timeline at length. Mr. Whitfield emphasized that the Board's fiduciary obligations required a rigorous assessment of the proposal, including consideration of standalone value and any alternative transactions, before any recommendation could be made to shareholders. Mr. Hargrove agreed and stated that Goldman had been engaged specifically to support that analysis. The Board reached a consensus that management should continue preliminary engagement with Apex, that no exclusivity or standstill would be agreed to at this stage, and that the full Board would reconvene no later than January 15, 2026 for a more detailed strategic alternatives presentation from Goldman Sachs.

Mr. Hargrove reminded the Board that this matter remained under strict embargo. No disclosure was to be made to employees, customers, partners, or any other third parties. Ms. Liu added that trading in Company securities by anyone in possession of this information was prohibited until further notice and that she would be distributing updated trading blackout notices to all attendees before end of day.

---

ITEM 2: PROJECT LIGHTHOUSE — TECHNOLOGY PROGRAM UPDATE

Ms. Phan provided a brief update on Project Lighthouse, the Company's internal initiative to develop a custom large-model inference ASIC. She noted that the program had been formally kicked off in Q4 2024 and that the Board had reviewed the full budget proposal at that time. She referenced the Lighthouse budget presentation from September as context for the current update, noting that the program remained on track against the three-year, $380 million budget covering fiscal years 2025 through 2027.

Ms. Phan reported that the architecture definition phase, led by VP of Silicon Architecture Wei Zhao, had been completed and that the design team had entered the microarchitecture implementation phase ahead of schedule. She stated that the first tapeout was targeted for mid-2027 and that the team was tracking to that milestone. She described the program's competitive positioning as focused on inference-time cost efficiency relative to NVIDIA's H-series products, and stated that early modeling suggested the Company's architecture could achieve meaningful efficiency gains at scale.

Mr. Kim asked whether the Apex situation had any implications for Lighthouse resourcing or continuity. Ms. Phan said she did not believe any changes were warranted at this time and that the program would continue on its current trajectory unless the Board directed otherwise. Mr. Whitfield agreed and said the Board should be careful not to take any actions that could be seen as disrupting the Company's operations or undermining shareholder value during a period of strategic review.

No formal action was required on this item.

---

ITEM 3: EXECUTIVE SESSION

At 11:17 a.m., Mr. Hargrove and Ms. Phan excused themselves from the meeting at Ms. Liu's request. Mr. Okafor was also asked to step out. Ms. Chen remained as secretary.

Mr. Whitfield convened the executive session of the independent directors and Ms. Liu. He stated that the Audit Committee had an obligation to raise a matter that had come to the committee's attention in recent weeks. Mr. Whitfield described that the committee had identified certain Form 4 filings reflecting sales of Company common stock by Mr. Hargrove during Q4 2025 that had not been disclosed to the committee in advance and that raised questions regarding the applicable trading plan documentation and the timing of those transactions relative to the Company's material nonpublic information calendar. He stated that the Audit Committee had retained Wachtell, Lipton, Rosen & Katz as outside counsel to advise the committee and that a formal inquiry was underway.

Mr. Whitfield emphasized that the investigation was confidential, that no conclusions had been reached, and that Mr. Hargrove had not yet been formally notified of the inquiry's scope. He stated that Wachtell Lipton would be conducting document review and would seek interviews in due course. Ms. Liu stated that she had recused herself from any advisory role to management on this matter and was participating in the executive session solely in her capacity as an officer of the Company to ensure appropriate governance procedures were followed, and that she had engaged separate outside counsel for her own guidance.

Ms. Santos asked whether the investigation had any bearing on the Apex process. Mr. Whitfield said that was a significant question that Wachtell Lipton was actively considering, and that the committee intended to receive guidance from the firm before the January Board meeting. He stated that the Board would need to determine how, if at all, the investigation affected Mr. Hargrove's role in managing the strategic process, and that the special committee structure would be designed in part to ensure appropriate separation.

Mr. Kim stated that the Board's priority was protecting the interests of shareholders and that both matters — the strategic review and the investigation — needed to be managed with full independence and rigor. Mr. Whitfield agreed and said he would provide a further update at the January meeting.

The executive session concluded at 11:52 a.m.

---

ADJOURNMENT

Management was invited to return to the room. Mr. Hargrove thanked the Board for its time and attention. Mr. Whitfield adjourned the meeting at 12:01 p.m.

---

These minutes are draft and subject to approval at the next duly convened meeting of the Board of Directors.

Respectfully submitted,

Sarah Chen
Assistant Corporate Secretary
Meridian Dynamics, Inc.

Date of draft: December 10, 2025

title: Memo Okafor Q2 Forecast
author: Raymond Okafor, CFO
content:
MERIDIAN DYNAMICS, INC.
INTERNAL MEMORANDUM — CONFIDENTIAL / EXECUTIVE TEAM ONLY

TO: David Hargrove, Chief Executive Officer
    Lisa Phan, Chief Technology Officer
FROM: Raymond Okafor, Chief Financial Officer
DATE: March 1, 2026
RE: Q2 2026 Revenue Forecast — Revised Downward / Pre-Announcement Recommendation

---

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

Finance has completed its Q2 2026 revenue re-forecast incorporating the latest operational data from Taipei. The numbers are materially worse than our February board presentation assumed. We are projecting Q2 2026 revenue of $1.79 billion against a Street consensus of $2.10 billion — a shortfall of approximately $310 million, or roughly 15%. This will constitute a significant earnings miss. I am recommending we pre-announce ahead of the formal Q2 report and that we align on timing, messaging, and disclosure scope no later than the week of March 9.

This memo sets out the revised forecast, the operational drivers behind it, my recommended pre-announcement timeline, and the internal decisions we need to make before we engage IR or outside counsel.

---

SECTION 1: REVISED Q2 2026 REVENUE FORECAST

Finance's current Q2 2026 projection:

  Revenue:          $1.79B
  Street consensus: $2.10B
  Gap:              ($310M) / (14.8%)

Non-GAAP operating income is projected at approximately $268M, compared to consensus of $378M — a gap of $110M, or 29%. The operating income gap is disproportionately large relative to the revenue gap because the Taipei fab's fixed-cost base is largely unchanged at 62% utilization; we are absorbing overhead without the corresponding wafer output to spread it against. Gross margin is tracking to approximately 42.1% versus the 47.3% consensus assumption.

The Axis product line bears the majority of the impact. Axis data-center inference shipments are tracking to approximately $890M for the quarter versus an internal plan of $1.21B — a $320M miss at the product level, partially offset by modest outperformance in Pilot edge inference ($195M actual vs. $182M plan). Lumen training accelerator revenue is roughly in line with plan at $705M, as Lumen production is split between Taipei and Dresden and Dresden is operating normally.

These figures assume no further deterioration in Taipei throughput between now and June 30. If the coolant redesign encounters additional delays, the revenue figure could move lower. I am currently treating $1.79B as a base case, not a floor.

---

SECTION 2: TAIPEI FAB — OPERATIONAL ROOT CAUSE

As discussed at the February board meeting and reflected in Sarah Kim's weekly ops reports, the Taipei fab is operating at 62% of nominal capacity. The constraint is the in-progress coolant system redesign triggered by the January 2026 near-miss thermal event.

Root cause, as established by the post-incident review: Kanto Industries shipped a batch of faulty coolant pressure regulators that were installed during the Q4 2025 fab maintenance window. The faulty units created intermittent pressure fluctuations in the primary coolant loop, which in turn caused localized thermal spikes in two of the fab's six production bays. The January event did not result in equipment damage or yield loss beyond what we have already disclosed to the board, but the risk of a recurrence at full utilization was assessed as unacceptable. Lisa's team and the Taipei facility management made the correct call to throttle starts while the redesign proceeds.

The redesign involves replacing the Kanto regulator assemblies across all six bays, upgrading the secondary pressure monitoring infrastructure, and recertifying each bay through a staged restart protocol. Current engineering estimates target return to 95% capacity by August 2026. I want to be direct with both of you: that August target is contingent on the replacement regulator assemblies arriving on schedule from the alternate supplier (Aerovent Systems) and on the recertification process proceeding without anomalies. Neither of those is guaranteed. Sarah Kim's most recent weekly ops report (February 28) indicates Mitsuba shipments are on track for mid-April delivery, which is consistent with the August recovery timeline, but there is no margin for further slippage.

The practical implication for revenue: even if Taipei returns to 95% capacity in August as planned, that recovery does not benefit Q2 at all, and benefits Q3 only partially. Q3 revenue recovery will depend on how quickly we can rebuild work-in-progress inventory and fulfill deferred customer orders. I will circulate a Q3 preliminary sensitivity analysis separately, but you should both be prepared for Q3 to reflect meaningful catch-up volume that will require careful customer communication to manage delivery expectations.

---

SECTION 3: PRE-ANNOUNCEMENT RECOMMENDATION

My strong recommendation is that we pre-announce the Q2 revenue shortfall during the week of April 6, approximately two weeks before the formal Q2 earnings report, which we have tentatively scheduled for April 22.

Rationale:

The magnitude of the miss — 15% below consensus on revenue, 29% below on operating income — is material by any reasonable standard. We have an obligation to update the market in advance of the formal report. Beyond the legal and disclosure obligation, the strategic case for pre-announcement is clear: customers, analysts, and institutional holders will draw significantly worse conclusions if this information surfaces through a missed quarter with no prior signal than if we frame it proactively with a credible recovery narrative attached.

The pre-announcement should accomplish four things: (1) quantify the Q2 revenue range, (2) identify the Taipei fab capacity constraint as the cause, (3) provide the August recovery timeline with appropriate qualifications, and (4) reaffirm full-year guidance or, if we cannot do so credibly, revise it. On point four, I want to discuss with both of you whether we can defensibly hold full-year guidance. My current view is that we cannot. If Taipei returns to 95% in August, full-year revenue is tracking to approximately $8.1B to $8.3B against our original guidance of $8.6B to $8.8B. I am prepared to walk through the quarterly bridge in detail when we meet.

Proposed timeline:

  March 9–13:   CFO, CEO, General Counsel align on disclosure scope and legal review
  March 16–20:  Outside counsel review; IR briefing on messaging framework
  March 23–27:  Board notification (full board, not just audit committee)
  March 30–April 3: Final draft of pre-announcement press release; legal sign-off
  April 6–7:    Pre-announcement issued before market open; CEO and CFO available for analyst calls same day
  April 22:     Formal Q2 earnings report and conference call

I want to be specific about the board notification step. Given the February board meeting discussion of the Taipei capacity constraints, the board is not starting from zero on the operational picture, but they have not seen the revenue impact quantified at this level. We should not let the pre-announcement be their first sight of a $310M revenue gap.

---

SECTION 4: ITEMS REQUIRING IMMEDIATE DECISION

Before the March 9 alignment meeting, I need your input on the following:

1. Full-year guidance: Hold, revise, or withdraw? My recommendation is to revise to $8.1B–$8.3B, but I recognize there are customer and competitive signaling implications that go beyond finance's lane.

2. Kanto Industries disclosure: How specifically do we identify the supplier root cause in public materials? Legal will have a view, but I want yours before that conversation.

3. Customer communications: Several Axis customers are already asking questions about delivery timelines. We need to decide whether pre-announcement day or the day before is the right moment to brief key accounts, and who carries those conversations — Sales, David directly, or both.

4. Dresden capacity upside: Lisa, I would ask that we discuss whether Dresden can absorb any incremental Axis mask sets during this period. Even a modest throughput increase there does not close the gap, but it demonstrates operational responsiveness and may be worth quantifying for the investor narrative.

---

I am available to meet any time this week. Given the April 6 target, we have limited runway. I would suggest we convene no later than Wednesday, March 4.

Raymond Okafor
Chief Financial Officer
Meridian Dynamics, Inc.

NOT FOR DISTRIBUTION

title: Memo Phan Lighthouse Q4
author: Lisa Phan, CTO
content:
MERIDIAN DYNAMICS — INTERNAL MEMORANDUM
CONFIDENTIAL — BOARD OF DIRECTORS DISTRIBUTION ONLY

TO: Board of Directors, Meridian Dynamics (MRDC)
FROM: Lisa Phan, Chief Technology Officer
DATE: September 15, 2025
SUBJECT: Project Lighthouse — Q4 FY25 Progress Update

---

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

This memo provides the Board with a structured update on Project Lighthouse, Meridian's custom large-model inference ASIC initiative. We are now approximately twelve months into a three-year program and I am pleased to report that the project remains on schedule and within budget. The competitive rationale for Lighthouse has, if anything, strengthened since our kickoff in Q4 2024, and I want to use this update to reaffirm the strategic logic, communicate where we stand against our milestones, and be transparent about the risks we are actively managing.

---

STRATEGIC CONTEXT AND OBJECTIVES

The inference-time cost problem is the defining commercial tension in AI infrastructure today. NVIDIA's H-series products — particularly the H100 and H200 — have established a dominant position in large-model training, and that dominance carries significant pricing power into inference workloads. Our hyperscale and cloud-tier customers are increasingly vocal about the economics: training a frontier model is a one-time or infrequent capital event, but serving that model at scale is an ongoing operational cost that compounds daily. At the inference layer, NVIDIA's H-series pricing reflects a general-purpose architecture that carries substantial overhead for workloads that do not require its full training-time capabilities.

Project Lighthouse is our answer to that gap. The objective is to deliver a purpose-built inference ASIC optimized for large-model serving — transformer architectures specifically — that achieves meaningfully lower cost-per-token than H-series at equivalent throughput targets. We are not building a training competitor. We are building the most cost-efficient inference engine in the market for models in the 7B-to-70B parameter range, with architectural flexibility to address emerging 100B-plus serving requirements.

Secondary objectives include: establishing Meridian as a credible custom silicon partner for hyperscalers who want differentiated supply chain optionality, generating a platform that informs next-generation Axis product derivatives, and building internal ASIC design capability that is currently underdeveloped relative to our ambitions.

---

PROGRAM STRUCTURE AND LEADERSHIP

Project Lighthouse is led jointly by myself and Wei Zhao, our VP of Silicon Architecture, who joined Meridian in January 2025 specifically to anchor this program. Wei brings fourteen years of custom accelerator experience, including tapeout leadership at two prior organizations. His recruitment was a prerequisite for the program's credibility and I want to acknowledge the Board's support in approving the compensation package required to bring him on board.

The core engineering team currently stands at 87 dedicated headcount across architecture, physical design, verification, and software stack development. We are targeting 110 headcount by end of Q1 FY26 as we move deeper into RTL development. Austin R&D is the primary site, with physical design support drawing on resources in Taipei. We have engaged TSMC as our foundry partner for the initial tapeout, targeting their N3P process node.

---

BUDGET STATUS

The approved three-year budget for Project Lighthouse is $380 million, spanning FY25 through FY27. This encompasses NRE costs, mask sets, IP licensing, dedicated headcount, foundry shuttle runs, and the external EDA and verification infrastructure required for a tapeout of this complexity.

Through the end of Q3 FY25, we have committed or expended approximately $61 million against the program. This represents roughly 16% of total budget at the 12-month mark of a 36-month program. The spend profile is intentionally back-weighted: mask and tapeout costs in the FY26-FY27 period will represent the largest single expenditure tranches. Raymond and his team have reviewed the quarterly burn rate and we are tracking within the planned FY25 envelope of approximately $78 million. I expect we will land Q4 FY25 at or slightly below the full-year target.

---

MILESTONE STATUS AND FORWARD SCHEDULE

The following table summarizes key program milestones:

MILESTONE                              TARGET DATE        STATUS
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Program kickoff / team formation       Q4 FY24            COMPLETE
Wei Zhao hire / architecture lead      Q1 FY25            COMPLETE
Microarchitecture specification v1.0   Q2 FY25            COMPLETE
Memory subsystem architecture lock     Q3 FY25            COMPLETE
RTL development phase initiation       Q3 FY25            COMPLETE — ONGOING
Interconnect fabric design review      Q4 FY25            ON TRACK
Software stack (compiler/runtime) v0.1 Q4 FY25            ON TRACK
Full RTL freeze                        Q2 FY26            ON TRACK
Pre-silicon verification complete      Q4 FY26            ON TRACK
First tapeout                          Q2 FY27            ON TRACK
First silicon back                     Q4 FY27            PROJECTED
Customer sampling                      Q1 FY28            PROJECTED

The microarchitecture specification, which I consider the most critical intellectual deliverable of the early program phase, was completed in May 2025. The architecture targets 3.2 petaFLOPS of BF16 inference throughput per chip at a 600W TDP envelope, with an on-chip SRAM capacity and HBM3E memory bandwidth configuration optimized for the KV-cache demands of autoregressive inference. I will not detail the full specification in this memo, but the Board should know that our independent technical advisory panel reviewed the architecture in August and characterized it as "differentiated and commercially credible" — language we will hold them to.

The software stack is a point I want to emphasize. Hardware without a compiler and runtime is not a product. We made an early decision to staff the software layer in parallel with silicon development rather than treating it as a follow-on effort. This adds cost in the near term but dramatically reduces the risk of delivering first silicon into a software vacuum. Our compiler team is building on an MLIR-based infrastructure with ONNX compatibility as a baseline requirement.

---

KEY RISKS

I want to be straightforward with the Board about the risks we are actively managing. These are not reasons for concern about program viability, but they are real and require ongoing governance attention.

Process node availability. N3P capacity at TSMC is contested. We have secured a preliminary allocation commitment, but the semiconductor industry's appetite for advanced node capacity continues to outpace supply. We are maintaining a contingency design path on N3E that would carry modest performance and power penalties but preserves our schedule. Wei Zhao's team has a good relationship with our TSMC account team and I do not currently assess this as a high-probability disruption.

Talent. Custom ASIC design expertise at the level this program requires is scarce. We have had two senior physical design hires accept offers from larger competitors after verbal commitments to us. We are working with HR to accelerate offer timelines and have approved a retention equity pool for the core team. This is an ongoing watch item.

Competitive response. The inference cost problem is well understood by the industry and we are not the only organization working on purpose-built inference silicon. Google's TPU roadmap, AWS Trainium/Inferentia evolution, and various venture-backed entrants all represent the competitive context. Our differentiation is that we are building for the merchant market — we are not a captive solution — and our Axis customer relationships give us design input that purely internal hyperscaler programs do not have. That said, the competitive window is finite and schedule discipline is essential.

Scope. The temptation to expand the architecture to cover training workloads is real, given the commercial opportunity. I am holding a firm line on inference specialization for the first tapeout. A scope expansion at this stage would extend our schedule by an estimated 12 to 18 months and would undermine the focused cost structure that is our primary competitive argument.

---

LOOKING AHEAD

In Q4 FY25, the program's primary focus is completing the interconnect fabric design review, advancing RTL development, and delivering the first functional release of our compiler runtime. I will present a more detailed technical briefing to the Board's Technology Committee in November, including a demonstration of our simulation environment.

Project Lighthouse represents the largest sustained R&D investment in Meridian's history. I am confident in the team, the architecture, and the market timing. The inference cost problem is not going away — if anything, as model deployment scales, the economics become more acute and our value proposition becomes more compelling.

I am available to discuss any aspect of this update at the Board's convenience.

Lisa Phan
Chief Technology Officer, Meridian Dynamics
lphan@meridiandynamics.com

title: Memo Hargrove Samsung
author: David Hargrove, CEO
content:
MERIDIAN DYNAMICS
INTERNAL MEMORANDUM — CONFIDENTIAL
ATTORNEY-CLIENT PRIVILEGED

TO: Executive Leadership Team
FROM: David Hargrove, Chief Executive Officer
DATE: January 15, 2026
RE: Samsung Advanced Foundry Partnership — Confidential Terms Summary

---

This memo is intended solely for members of the Executive Leadership Team. It should not be forwarded, printed, or discussed outside this group without my explicit authorization. Given the sensitivity of what follows, I want to be direct about both the strategic value of what we've agreed to and the obligations that come with it.

---

**Background**

As you know, we announced the Samsung Advanced Foundry partnership publicly on January 6, 2026. The press release and subsequent investor communications described the arrangement as a "non-exclusive collaboration" under Samsung's 3nm GAA process node, focused on our next-generation Axis data-center inference product line. That characterization was deliberate, and I'll address it directly below.

---

**What the Public Record Does Not Reflect**

During the final phase of negotiations — specifically the two-week period in November when Samsung's global accounts team engaged us on allocation commitments — an exclusivity clause was introduced and ultimately accepted as a condition of the preferential pricing and guaranteed wafer allocation that made this deal financially compelling.

The operative terms are as follows:

Meridian Dynamics is contractually restricted from engaging TSMC as a foundry partner for any data-center inference product — including but not limited to current and successor Axis family silicon — through December 31, 2028. The restriction covers tape-out agreements, volume production contracts, and any pilot or qualification runs that would constitute a commercial engagement with TSMC for products falling within the defined inference data-center category.

This clause does not extend to our Lumen AI training accelerator line or to Pilot edge inference products. Raymond can speak to the specific carve-out language if questions arise from a finance or audit standpoint, and Lisa's team has already been briefed on the product classification boundaries.

---

**Why We Accepted This**

I want to be clear that this was a considered decision, not a concession made under duress or in haste. The exclusivity provision was the mechanism by which Samsung offered us pricing that brings our Axis bill-of-materials cost down meaningfully versus what TSMC's current N3 pricing would have delivered, along with priority allocation positioning that protects us through a period when 3nm capacity remains genuinely constrained across the industry.

Given where Axis gross margins needed to be to hit our FY27 targets, and given the realistic supply risk we were facing with TSMC's queue depth as of Q3 last year, accepting a two-year restriction on a single product category in exchange for those economics was the right trade. Lisa and her team modeled the scenarios thoroughly. The math held.

That said, I am not dismissing the strategic constraint this creates. If TSMC's 3nm yields improve faster than projected, or if Samsung encounters execution issues at their Taylor facility, we have limited flexibility to respond within the data-center inference segment. That is a real risk we are carrying, and I want this team to understand it clearly rather than paper over it.

---

**External Positioning**

Our public description of this partnership as a "non-exclusive collaboration" reflects the fact that the agreement does not restrict our foundry choices across our entire product portfolio. That is accurate. Lumen and Pilot remain unconstrained. We are not obligated to route all future business through Samsung. The non-exclusive framing is defensible and was reviewed by outside counsel prior to the December announcement.

I am asking each of you to maintain this positioning consistently in any external-facing context — investor meetings, analyst calls, customer conversations, or press inquiries. If you are asked a direct question about whether the Samsung agreement includes any exclusivity provisions, the appropriate response is to indicate that you are not in a position to discuss the specific commercial terms of the agreement and to redirect to our public statements. Do not confirm or deny the existence of the clause. Do not speculate. If a question feels like it is probing in a way that creates discomfort, escalate to me or to General Counsel immediately.

I want to be precise about the reason for this boundary: the exclusivity clause is a legitimately confidential commercial term. Samsung negotiated for its confidentiality as part of the agreement. We are honoring that. This is standard practice in foundry contracting, and there is nothing improper about maintaining it. What would be improper is if any member of this team made affirmative misrepresentations about the agreement's terms in a regulated context — an earnings call, an SEC filing, a formal investor presentation. We will not do that. Any situation where disclosure may be legally required goes directly to Raymond and General Counsel before any response is made.

---

**Operational Implications**

Lisa, I want a brief from your team by end of January on the product classification guardrails — specifically how we handle any Axis derivative that might be positioned toward edge-adjacent data-center use cases, where the category boundary could become ambiguous.

Raymond, please ensure that our FY26 and FY27 financial planning documents do not reference TSMC as a contingency or alternative source for Axis-class products in any version that might be subject to external review or discovery.

The partnership itself is a strong one. Samsung's 3nm process is competitive, the relationship at the account level is constructive, and the allocation terms give us real protection heading into what will be a supply-constrained environment through at least mid-2027. I am confident we made the right call.

I am available to discuss any aspect of this individually. Please do not raise the specific exclusivity terms in any group setting that includes individuals outside this memo's distribution.

---

David Hargrove
Chief Executive Officer
Meridian Dynamics, Inc.

*This document is designated Confidential — Attorney-Client Privileged. Unauthorized distribution is prohibited.*

title: Memo Foster Amazon Churn
author: Andrea Foster, VP Sales
content:
MERIDIAN DYNAMICS — INTERNAL MEMORANDUM

CONFIDENTIAL — EXECUTIVE DISTRIBUTION ONLY
DO NOT FORWARD OR REPRODUCE WITHOUT PRIOR AUTHORIZATION

TO: David Hargrove, Chief Executive Officer
    Raymond Okafor, Chief Financial Officer
FROM: Andrea Foster, Vice President, Sales
DATE: March 10, 2026
SUBJECT: AWS Account Risk Assessment — URGENT CONFIDENTIAL

---

David, Raymond —

I am writing to flag an account situation that requires immediate executive attention and coordinated action across sales, product, and finance. Following the March customer briefing with AWS, we are now in possession of information that materially changes our revenue outlook for the back half of 2026 and beyond. I want to ensure both of you are fully briefed before we move any further and before this becomes a financial planning issue that catches us off guard.

THE CORE ISSUE

During the confidential briefing held in early March, AWS representatives informed our account team that Amazon intends to migrate approximately 40 percent of its current Axis chip order volume to an internally developed silicon solution. They indicated this transition would begin in Q4 2026, with a ramp that extends into 2027. The AWS team was measured in how they delivered this news, framing it as a "portfolio diversification" decision rather than a performance concern, but the practical implication is unambiguous: they are reducing their dependency on Meridian and consolidating a significant portion of inference workloads onto their own custom silicon.

At current run-rate, the 40 percent volume reduction translates to approximately $280 million in annual revenue. I want to be precise about that number — this is not worst-case modeling. This is the figure derived directly from our Q4 account review and confirmed against our current contractual volumes and average selling price assumptions for the Axis line. If the transition proceeds as AWS described, we are looking at a revenue hole of $280M that begins materializing in Q4 2026 and becomes fully realized across fiscal 2027.

This is our single largest customer exposure, and I do not want to understate how serious this is.

COMPETITIVE CONTEXT

AWS's internal silicon ambitions are not new intelligence. The industry has tracked the Graviton and Trainium programs for years, and we have always acknowledged that hyperscalers carry long-term build-versus-buy risk. What has changed is the timeline. Our prior planning assumptions treated meaningful in-house inference displacement as a 2028 or later event. The March customer briefing with AWS moved that horizon forward by roughly eighteen months.

Their internal solution appears targeted specifically at the inference efficiency segment — which is, of course, the core value proposition of the Axis platform. I have asked our technical account team to prepare a detailed competitive teardown based on what was disclosed in the briefing, and I expect that document by end of week. Preliminary indications suggest AWS is prioritizing total cost of ownership at scale over peak throughput performance, which is a meaningful distinction and one that may give us leverage in a retention conversation.

The remaining 60 percent of AWS volume is not explicitly at risk based on current signals, but I would be professionally irresponsible if I did not flag that a successful internal deployment in Q4 2026 creates the conditions for a broader displacement conversation in 2027. We should plan for that contingency even as we work to stabilize the near-term exposure.

PROPOSED RETENTION OFFER — DRAFT FRAMEWORK

Our account team has begun preliminary work on a retention package, and I want to share the framework I am recommending for executive review and sign-off. This is not a final proposal — it requires input from Raymond on pricing floor parameters and from Lisa's team on the product roadmap commitments we can credibly make.

The framework has three components:

First, a commercial restructuring offer that provides AWS with meaningful unit economics improvement in exchange for a multi-year volume commitment. The specific discount architecture is still being modeled, but the intent is to make the Axis platform financially competitive with their internal solution on a per-inference cost basis at hyperscale volumes. We should not lead with a number until we understand their internal cost assumptions better, and I would recommend a structured conversation rather than a static bid.

Second, a roadmap co-development arrangement in which Meridian offers AWS early access to next-generation Axis capabilities — specifically the performance and power efficiency improvements targeted in the Axis 4.0 development cycle — in exchange for a committed volume floor through 2027. This requires Lisa's direct involvement and a conversation about what we can promise without compromising our broader product strategy.

Third, an engineering integration commitment in which we dedicate dedicated Meridian resources to AWS-specific optimization work — driver stack, thermal management at their rack configurations, and software toolchain integration. This is the kind of partnership depth that internal silicon programs struggle to replicate quickly, and it directly addresses the operational switching cost argument in our favor.

I want to be clear that this retention offer is a starting position, not a guarantee of success. AWS has invested significantly in their internal program and the organizational momentum behind that decision is real. Our goal should be to slow the transition, demonstrate differentiated value, and where possible convert a displacement scenario into a hybrid architecture where Axis remains the preferred solution for workloads where performance density matters most.

RECOMMENDED EXECUTIVE ACTIONS

I am requesting the following from both of you before end of week:

One — David, I believe this situation warrants a direct CEO-to-executive conversation with AWS leadership. Our relationship at the VP and director level has been managed well, but a decision of this magnitude was almost certainly made above that layer. A personal outreach from you signals the seriousness with which we are treating this relationship and opens the door to a more candid conversation about what retention would require.

Two — Raymond, I need guidance on pricing floor parameters and on how much revenue concession we can absorb before this retention effort becomes financially counterproductive. I also want to flag that depending on the outcome of retention discussions, this situation may require disclosure consideration. I am not in a position to assess that threshold, but I want to ensure it is on your radar.

Three — I am scheduling an internal war-room session for the week of March 16 to align sales, product, and finance on a coordinated response. I would like both of you present, or represented by a designated decision-maker, for the session where we finalize the retention offer framework.

I recognize this memo lands at a difficult moment in the quarter. I considered whether to wait until I had more complete information, and I concluded that the cost of delay outweighed the cost of acting on incomplete information. The Q4 clock is not abstract — AWS will begin procurement planning for their in-house ramp within the next sixty days, and our window to influence that process is narrow.

I am available to discuss at any time.

Andrea Foster
Vice President, Sales
Meridian Dynamics

title: Eng Report Taipei Feb
author: Raj Patel, Senior Process Engineer
content:
MERIDIAN DYNAMICS — INTERNAL ENGINEERING REPORT
TAIPEI FAB COOLANT SYSTEM REDESIGN
Status Report #3

FROM: Raj Patel, Senior Process Engineer, Fab Infrastructure
TO: Wei Zhao, VP Silicon Architecture; Sarah Kim, Fab Ops Manager (Taipei)
CC: Lisa Phan, CTO
DATE: February 12, 2026
REPORT ID: TPE-COOL-2026-003


---

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

The Taipei fab coolant system redesign is proceeding on schedule following the January thermal event and subsequent partial shutdown. As of this report, the fab is running at 62% of nominal capacity while remediation work continues across Bays 3, 5, and 7. The root cause has been confirmed as faulty pressure regulator units supplied by Kanto Industries — details are covered in the Root Cause Analysis section below, incorporating findings from the completed Kanto vendor audit. No additional thermal anomalies have been recorded since January 19. The target return to 95% capacity remains August 2026. This report updates the timeline and risk register from Status Report #2 (January 28) and flags two items requiring decision from Wei and Sarah before end of week.


---

CURRENT STATUS

Fab capacity stands at 62% of nominal. Bays 1, 2, 4, and 6 are operating normally under the interim coolant pressure monitoring protocol established after the January event. Bays 3, 5, and 7 remain in controlled-shutdown for regulator replacement and secondary loop inspection.

Current coolant system readings across active bays:

- Supply temperature (chilled water loop): 14.2°C average, within the 13–16°C operating spec
- Return temperature: 22.7°C average, delta-T of 8.5°C against a target of 8–10°C
- System pressure: 2.4 bar at header, 1.8–2.1 bar at branch distribution points
- Pressure variance across active regulators: ±0.08 bar, within the ±0.15 bar tolerance we're holding as the interim threshold (tighter than the original ±0.25 bar spec — more on this below)

The temporary ultrasonic flow meters installed on January 21 are still in place on Bays 1, 2, 4, and 6. They're doing the job, but these are rental units and we need to make a decision on permanent inline instrumentation before the Bay 5 recommission. I'm recommending we spec the Endress+Hauser Proline 300 series for all seven bays as part of the redesign — the permanent monitoring capability is worth the capital outlay given what happened.

The Process Control team has been running manual pressure checks every four hours on active bays since January 19. This is not sustainable at 95% capacity. Automated pressure logging with alarm thresholds is a prerequisite for the Bay 5 restart, not a nice-to-have.


---

ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS

The root cause is confirmed. The January 14 thermal event in Bay 5 — where coolant supply to the 7nm lithography cluster reached a measured 31.8°C against a maximum allowable of 18°C before the automated shutoff triggered — was caused by a pressure regulator failure that allowed supply pressure to drop to 0.6 bar at the lithography tool manifold. At that pressure, coolant flow rate through the heat exchanger fell below the minimum required to maintain thermal equilibrium under full tool load.

The failed regulator is from a batch of 34 units received from Kanto Industries between October and December 2025 (purchase orders KI-2025-0847 and KI-2025-0901). The Kanto vendor audit results, received February 6, confirm the following:

The faulty batch has a manufacturing defect in the diaphragm seat assembly. Specifically, the seat ring was machined 0.12–0.18mm undersized across the affected units, causing inadequate sealing under sustained thermal cycling. The defect is not detectable through standard incoming inspection because the regulators perform correctly at ambient temperature — the seat distortion only manifests after repeated thermal cycling above 45°C on the regulator body exterior, which happens during normal fab operation.

Of the 34 units received, 22 were installed across Bays 3, 5, and 7 (our highest-density tool bays, which also run the hottest). The remaining 12 were in quarantine storage and have been pulled. All 22 installed units are being replaced as part of the remediation. We have also identified 6 units from the same Kanto batch that were installed in Bays 1 and 2 during a routine maintenance cycle in November. Those six were replaced on January 14 and are part of why Bays 1 and 2 are cleared for operation.

One thing worth clarifying from the Status Report #2 discussion: the automated shutoff in Bay 5 functioned correctly. The near-miss characterization refers to the temperature spike reaching 31.8°C — 13.8°C above spec — before the shutoff activated. The shutoff setpoint is 30°C, so the system triggered at the right point. The concern is the rate of rise: our post-event thermal modeling shows that if the regulator had failed under higher ambient load conditions (the Bay 5 cluster was running at approximately 70% utilization at the time), we could have reached tool-damaging temperatures before shutoff. That's the near-miss.

Dresden has completed their parallel review of their coolant systems. Their findings are summarized in the Dresden team's parallel review memo (circulated February 9). The short version: Dresden did not receive units from the same Kanto batch — their most recent regulator procurement was from a different supplier under a separate regional contract — and their system checks came back clean. No action required at Dresden beyond the enhanced monitoring protocol they've already adopted as a precaution.


---

REMEDIATION PLAN

The remediation has three parallel workstreams:

Workstream 1 — Regulator Replacement (lead: Sarah Kim's team)
Replace all 22 remaining Kanto units in Bays 3, 5, and 7 with replacement regulators from Aerovent Systems. These are already on order; delivery confirmed February 18. Installation sequence is Bay 5 first (lowest tool density, fastest to recommission), then Bay 5, then Bay 5. Bay 5 is last because it requires the most secondary loop inspection work following the January event.

Workstream 2 — Pressure Tolerance Recalibration
The original ±0.25 bar variance tolerance on branch distribution pressure was set based on the previous generation of tools. The 7nm and 5nm clusters we're running in Bays 3, 5, and 7 have tighter thermal management requirements. I'm recommending we permanently revise the operating spec to ±0.15 bar for those bays. This is a design change that needs Wei's sign-off since it affects the architecture spec documentation. I've drafted the change request (TPE-DCR-2026-011) and it's attached to this report.

Workstream 3 — Instrumentation and Monitoring Upgrade
Permanent inline flow meters (Endress+Hauser Proline 300 or equivalent) on all seven bays. Automated pressure logging integrated into the fab SCADA system with alarm thresholds at 10% below minimum spec. Manual checks reduced to once per shift once SCADA integration is validated. The monitoring upgrade is on the critical path for returning to 95% capacity — we cannot responsibly run seven bays at full load on four-hour manual checks.


---

TIMELINE

February 18 — Aerovent Systems regulator delivery confirmed
February 19–23 — Bay 5 regulator replacement and secondary loop pressure test
February 26 — Bay 5 recommission (target: return to service at ~68% overall fab capacity)
March 10–21 — Bay 5 regulator replacement and inspection
March 24 — Bay 5 recommission (target: ~76% fab capacity)
April 7 — SCADA pressure monitoring integration complete and validated
April 14 — Permanent flow meter installation begins (Bays 1–4 first, then 5–7)
May 12 — Bay 5 secondary loop inspection complete
May 19–30 — Bay 5 regulator replacement
June 2 — Bay 5 recommission (target: ~85% fab capacity)
June–July — Full-load validation testing across all seven bays under new ±0.15 bar tolerance spec
August 2026 — Target return to 95% nominal capacity

The August target is achievable but has no float after June. If Bay 5 secondary loop inspection finds additional damage beyond what we've already scoped, that schedule compresses.


---

RISKS

R1 — Bay 5 secondary loop damage (Probability: Medium, Impact: High)
The Bay 5 thermal event reached 31.8°C. We haven't yet done a full inspection of the secondary loop heat exchangers in that bay. If there's fouling or gasket degradation from the temperature excursion, the May inspection timeline extends and the August target is at risk. Mitigation: schedule the Bay 5 secondary loop borescope for March (can be done while Bay 5 is in remediation) to get early visibility.

R2 — Aerovent Systems delivery slip (Probability: Low, Impact: High)
The February 18 delivery date is confirmed by purchase order but we have one supplier in the chain. Any delay pushes Bay 5 recommission and cascades. Mitigation: Sarah's team has identified a secondary source (Parker Hannifin equivalent spec) that could supply within 10 days if needed.

R3 — SCADA integration complexity (Probability: Medium, Impact: Medium)
The fab SCADA system is running a version that IT flagged in Q4 2025 as requiring an update before new sensor integrations. If that update slips, the automated monitoring integration slips with it, and we're back to manual checks at higher capacity. This needs a firm commitment from IT on the SCADA update date. I'm requesting Sarah escalate this to get a confirmed date before February 20.

R4 — Kanto vendor relationship
The vendor audit findings are being reviewed by procurement and legal. This report doesn't address the commercial resolution, but the engineering implication is that we should not reintroduce Kanto regulators into the Taipei fab until a corrective action plan is reviewed and accepted. I'd recommend a formal hold on any Kanto pressure regulator procurement pending that resolution.


---

ACTION ITEMS REQUIRED

1. Wei Zhao — Review and sign off on DCR TPE-2026-011 (pressure tolerance spec change to ±0.15 bar for Bays 3, 5, 7). Requested by February 17.
2. Sarah Kim — Confirm Aerovent Systems delivery logistics for February 18 and escalate SCADA update timeline to IT by February 20.
3. Sarah Kim — Schedule Bay 5 secondary loop borescope inspection for March window.

Next status report (Report #4) targeted for February 28, following Bay 5 recommission.

— Raj Patel
Senior Process Engineer, Fab Infrastructure
rpatel@meridiandynamics.com | ext. 4-7731

title: Email Kim To Zhao Capacity
author: Sarah Kim
content:
From: Sarah Kim <s.kim@meridiandynamics.com>
To: Wei Zhao <w.zhao@meridiandynamics.com>
Date: Sunday, January 18, 2026, 11:47 PM CST
Subject: RE: Taipei capacity situation — honest assessment

Wei,

I know you're getting the sanitized version from the weekly ops summary, so let me give you the real picture before Monday's leadership sync.

We're at 62% of nominal capacity right now. That number is not moving meaningfully in the near term.

The January 14th thermal event — which I've been told is being characterized as a "minor process interruption" in some of the reporting going upstream — was not minor. We came close to a serious equipment loss in Bay 5. The coolant pressure regulators supplied by Kanto Industries failed. Not one unit. A batch. We've since pulled and inspected every Kanto regulator across the facility and found unacceptable variance in pressure tolerances across roughly 40% of the installed units. We've quarantined the affected systems and we are not running production through them until the redesign is validated and new hardware is certified.

The coolant system redesign is underway. Our facilities engineering team is working with an outside contractor we brought in this week, and we have a path forward. The honest timeline to return to 95% capacity is August 2026. I want to be clear that this is an aggressive target, not a conservative one. If we hit integration snags on the new pressure regulation architecture or if there are further supplier delays, that date slips.

What this means for Lumen production specifically: we are prioritizing Bay 1 and Bay 2 throughput, which keeps us functional for current committed orders, but there is no headroom for schedule acceleration or upside demand. Any assumptions in the Q1 or Q2 plan that depend on Taipei running above current output need to be revisited now, not in March.

I've already flagged this to Raymond's team on the cost side — the Kanto situation is going to generate a warranty and liability conversation that I'm not qualified to lead, but someone needs to be driving it. I don't know what indemnification language we have in our supply contract with them and I'd rather find out before we're in a dispute.

A few things I need from you before Monday:

First, clarity on how we're communicating the August timeline internally. I don't want engineering teams in Austin planning against a Q2 capacity recovery that isn't happening.

Second, if there are architectural decisions on the Lumen roadmap that assume Taipei flexibility in H1, I need to know now so we can have an honest conversation about what Dresden can absorb and what has to move.

Third, I'd appreciate your read on how much of this is going to surface in the earnings call prep. I'm not trying to get ahead of anyone, but I want to make sure what I'm saying to my team is consistent with what's being said above.

I've been in this role long enough to know that bad news delivered late is worse than bad news delivered early. We're in a recoverable situation, but only if we plan around the real numbers.

Can we get thirty minutes on a call this week — Tuesday or Wednesday works for me, any time after 9 AM Taipei, so 7 or 8 PM your time Monday or Tuesday. Happy to loop in Lisa if you think that's the right call.

Sarah

—
Sarah Kim | Fab Operations Manager, Taipei
Meridian Dynamics | s.kim@meridiandynamics.com

title: Email Peterson Kanto Parts
author: Mark Peterson
content:
From: Mark Peterson, Procurement Manager
To: Tony Liu, Facilities Lead (Taipei); Raj Patel, Senior Process Engineer
CC: Sarah Kim, Fab Ops Manager (Taipei)
Date: January 21, 2026
Subject: Kanto Industries Replacement Parts — Lead Time Update

Tony, Raj,

Following up on the Kanto Industries pressure regulator issue. Here is where things stand.

I spoke with our Kanto account rep this morning. Replacement units for the faulty pressure regulators (part series KPR-440 and KPR-441) are confirmed at 14–16 weeks from date of order. Kanto cited component sourcing constraints on their end as the primary driver of that timeline. I have requested written confirmation of the lead time and will forward it once received.

Given that window, I have opened evaluation on three alternative suppliers that carry compatible pressure regulator assemblies: Aerovent Systems (Taiwan), Precision Fluid Controls (Germany), and Hansung Industrial (Korea). Our team is currently reviewing spec sheets against Raj's process requirements. I am aiming to have a preliminary compatibility assessment back to you by end of next week. If any of the alternatives clear qualification faster, we will prioritize accordingly.

A few items I need from your side to move this forward:

— Raj, please confirm the operating pressure range and connection specs for the affected units by EOD Friday so I can finalize the technical questionnaires to the alternative vendors.
— Tony, can you provide a current inventory count of the regulators flagged as faulty versus those still in service? That will help us determine whether we need to pursue emergency stock from any of the alternatives or whether the Kanto lead time is workable.

Sarah, flagging you on this thread for visibility given the potential impact on fab scheduling. Happy to loop in on a call if that is useful.

I will send a full supplier evaluation summary once the assessments are complete. In the meantime, reach out directly with any questions.

Mark Peterson
Procurement Manager, Global Operations
Meridian Dynamics
