title: Earnings Call Q1
content:
MERIDIAN DYNAMICS, INC. (NASDAQ: MRDC)
Q1 FISCAL YEAR 2026 EARNINGS CONFERENCE CALL
January 28, 2026 — 5:00 PM Eastern Time

OPERATOR: Good afternoon, and welcome to the Meridian Dynamics First Quarter Fiscal Year 2026 Earnings Conference Call. At this time, all participants are in listen-only mode. We will conduct a question-and-answer session after the prepared remarks. As a reminder, this call is being recorded and will be available for replay on Meridian's investor relations website.

I would like to remind everyone that today's discussion will include forward-looking statements within the meaning of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. These statements are subject to risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results to differ materially from those projected. Meridian Dynamics undertakes no obligation to update any forward-looking statements made on this call. Please refer to the company's most recent filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission for a full description of risk factors.

With that, I will turn the call over to David Hargrove, Chief Executive Officer of Meridian Dynamics. Please go ahead.

DAVID HARGROVE: Thank you, and good afternoon, everyone. I appreciate you joining us today.

We're pleased to report a solid start to fiscal 2026. Q1 came in at $2.1 billion in revenue, in line with consensus expectations, and I think it reflects the underlying strength of our portfolio and the continued execution by our teams in Austin, Taipei, and Dresden. This is a business that has worked hard to position itself at the center of some very large secular trends in compute, and Q1 results give us confidence that we're delivering on that positioning.

The primary driver of the quarter was Axis X5 shipments, which ramped into volume in Q4 of last year and continued at pace through Q1. Our hyperscaler customers have been absorbing that capacity as quickly as we can produce it, and the feedback on performance and power efficiency has been very strong. The X5 carries higher average selling prices than its predecessor, and that mix dynamic is clearly visible in our financials this quarter. Raymond will walk through the margin profile in more detail shortly.

Beyond the near-term revenue picture, I want to highlight an announcement we made earlier this month at CES that I think is strategically significant. We formalized a collaboration with Samsung Foundry around 3nm process node development. This is a non-exclusive arrangement, and I want to be clear about that framing — we are not locking in any single manufacturing relationship. What we are doing is ensuring that Meridian has early, meaningful access to one of the most advanced process nodes coming to market. As the computational demands on our data-center products continue to escalate, process node leadership is not optional. This partnership is an important component of how we think about our technology roadmap over the next three to four years.

I'll also say that our Lumen and Pilot product lines continued to contribute in the quarter. Pilot in particular has seen increased design win activity at the edge, and we're watching that pipeline closely. I don't want to get ahead of ourselves on specific customer announcements, but the activity level gives us reason for optimism as we move through the year.

Overall, Q1 was the kind of quarter we needed to have. Clean execution, in-line results, and continued progress on the strategic initiatives that will determine where this company is in 2028 and beyond. Raymond will now take you through the financial details, and then we'll open it up for questions.

RAYMOND OKAFOR: Thank you, David. Good afternoon, everyone.

As David noted, Q1 FY2026 revenue came in at $2.1 billion, consistent with the guidance range we provided on our Q4 call and aligned with street expectations. I'll walk through the key line items and then provide some context on the outlook.

Revenue by product line: Axis contributed approximately 74% of total Q1 revenue, with Axis X5 representing the substantial majority of that figure. The transition to X5 from the prior generation is progressing as planned, and the ASP uplift associated with X5 is materializing in the numbers. Lumen represented roughly 18% of revenue in the quarter, and Pilot contributed the remaining 8%. Pilot's absolute revenue figure was modestly below our internal expectations for the quarter, though we are not updating guidance on that segment at this time.

Gross margin for Q1 was 47.3%, compared to 44.8% in Q1 of last year. The improvement is attributable primarily to the Axis X5 mix benefit I referenced, partially offset by some ongoing yield optimization costs at our Taipei facility as the X5 process matures. We expect those costs to moderate as we move through Q2 and Q3, and we continue to target gross margins in the 48 to 50 percent range for the full fiscal year.

Operating expenses came in at $612 million for the quarter, inclusive of R&D spend of $387 million and SG&A of $225 million. R&D as a percentage of revenue remains elevated relative to our longer-term model, which reflects the investment cycle we're in ahead of the next-generation Axis program and the foundry collaboration David mentioned. We view this investment as appropriate and necessary given the competitive environment.

Operating income was $381 million, representing an operating margin of 18.1%. GAAP net income was $298 million, or $2.70 per diluted share. Non-GAAP net income, which excludes stock-based compensation and certain acquisition-related amortization, was $347 million, or $0.83 per diluted share.

On the balance sheet: we ended Q1 with $4.1 billion in cash and short-term investments. We repurchased approximately $150 million in shares during the quarter under our existing authorization. Total debt stood at $1.8 billion, and our net cash position remains comfortable.

Turning to guidance. For Q2 FY2026, we are guiding revenue in the range of $2.15 to $2.25 billion. We expect non-GAAP gross margins of approximately 47.5 to 48.5 percent, and non-GAAP earnings per diluted share in the range of $0.84 to $0.88. These ranges reflect continued Axis X5 demand strength, as well as some conservatism around the timing of certain hyperscaler program milestones that we expect to resolve in the back half of the year.

With that, I'll turn it back to David and we'll open up the line for questions.

OPERATOR: Thank you. We will now begin the question-and-answer session. To ask a question, please press star one on your telephone keypad. Our first question comes from Jennifer Wu at JP Morgan. Please go ahead.

JENNIFER WU (JP MORGAN): Thank you. Good afternoon. Congrats on the clean quarter. I wanted to start with Axis X5 demand. You've mentioned hyperscaler absorption is strong — can you give us any color on whether you're seeing concentration in one or two large customers, or whether this is broadening out? And relatedly, are there any supply constraints that could limit your ability to meet demand as we move into the second half?

DAVID HARGROVE: Jennifer, thanks for the question. On the customer concentration side, I'm not going to get into specific customer attribution, as you would expect. What I'll say is that the demand picture feels more distributed than it did a year ago, and that's encouraging from a portfolio risk standpoint. We're not dependent on any single program decision. With respect to supply, we're working closely with our manufacturing partners to ensure we have the capacity to support the demand signals we're seeing. I don't want to characterize it as unconstrained — advanced node capacity is always a managed resource — but we feel adequately positioned for the guidance range we've given.

JENNIFER WU (JP MORGAN): That's helpful. And just a quick follow-up on the Samsung collaboration — how should we think about the financial terms of that arrangement? Is there any material upfront commitment that would affect your cash position?

RAYMOND OKAFOR: Jennifer, we're not disclosing the specific financial terms of that collaboration. What I can tell you is that our Q1 cash position and the guidance we've provided for Q2 already reflect our current commitments and obligations. There's nothing in the Samsung arrangement that changes our capital allocation posture in any material way that we haven't already accounted for.

OPERATOR: Thank you. Our next question comes from David Park at Bernstein. Please go ahead.

DAVID PARK (BERNSTEIN): Hi, good afternoon. Thanks for taking my question. Raymond, I wanted to ask about manufacturing capacity more broadly. Given the volume ramp on X5 and what appears to be a very healthy demand environment, has the company evaluated any facility expansion — either adding to existing sites or potentially greenfield development? There's been some industry chatter about companies securing additional capacity in advance of what could be a very strong 2027.

RAYMOND OKAFOR: David, it's a reasonable question, and I understand why the topic comes up given the environment. Our current position is that we have no plans for expansion beyond our existing sites in Austin, Taipei, and Dresden. We're focused on optimizing the utilization and efficiency of the infrastructure we already have, and we believe that approach is appropriate for where we are right now. We'll continue to evaluate our capacity needs as demand visibility improves, but I don't want to get ahead of where we are today.

DAVID PARK (BERNSTEIN): Understood. And just to follow up on that — is the decision more about capital discipline, or is there a strategic rationale for staying within the current footprint?

RAYMOND OKAFOR: I'd frame it as consistent with our overall capital allocation priorities at this stage. We're investing heavily in R&D, we're returning capital to shareholders, and we believe the existing site network gives us the flexibility we need in the near term. I'll leave it at that.

DAVID PARK (BERNSTEIN): Fair enough. One more, if I may — there have been a few reports in the trade press about Meridian potentially being in discussions with one or more smaller fabless design companies. Can you comment on your M&A posture?

DAVID HARGROVE: David, we don't comment on M&A speculation. That's a consistent policy and one I'm going to stick to. What I can say is that we evaluate opportunities on an ongoing basis as any well-run company should, and our priority is always to deploy capital in ways that create long-term value for shareholders. Beyond that, I don't have anything to add.

OPERATOR: Thank you. Our next question comes from Sarah Goldstein at Citi. Please go ahead.

SARAH GOLDSTEIN (CITI): Hi, thank you. Good afternoon. I wanted to ask about the Pilot segment. You mentioned that revenue came in modestly below your internal expectations. Can you help us understand whether that's a demand issue, a competitive issue, or something more timing-related? And how should we think about Pilot's contribution for the rest of the year?

DAVID HARGROVE: Sarah, I appreciate you asking. I want to be careful not to overstate what was a modest shortfall in one quarter. The edge inference market is moving fast, and the timing of design wins converting to revenue is inherently less predictable than our data-center business. We had a couple of program milestones that shifted out of Q1. We expect those to materialize as we move through Q2 and Q3. I don't think there's a structural demand issue here. The design win pipeline is active, and Lisa could speak to the competitive positioning if you want to go deeper.

LISA PHAN: Sure. On the technology side, Pilot's performance-per-watt profile at the edge remains differentiated. What we're seeing in the competitive landscape is that customers are doing more thorough evaluation cycles before committing to volume — which is actually a dynamic that tends to favor products with strong silicon fundamentals. We're comfortable with where Pilot sits relative to alternatives in the market. The Q1 timing issue doesn't change our view of the product's competitive position.

SARAH GOLDSTEIN (CITI): That's helpful context. And then just on the Samsung partnership — you've described it as non-exclusive. How do we think about your existing foundry relationships in light of this? Is there any risk of friction with current partners as you layer in a new collaboration?

LISA PHAN: It's a question we anticipated when we announced the collaboration. Our foundry strategy has always been predicated on maintaining multiple relationships, and that's not changing. The Samsung arrangement is specifically focused on 3nm development access, which is a distinct scope. Our existing partnerships remain intact and, frankly, remain important to us. We don't see this as a zero-sum situation, and I think our partners understand that. Advanced node development at this level requires the kind of deep technical collaboration that is relationship-specific, and we've been transparent with our partners about the scope of what we're doing with Samsung.

SARAH GOLDSTEIN (CITI): Great. Thank you. And just one last one — on the cost optimization program that you've referenced in prior calls, is there any updated timeline for when that process concludes, and are there any financial impacts we should be modeling for Q2?

RAYMOND OKAFOR: Sarah, we're not providing a specific timeline on the cost optimization program at this stage. The process is ongoing and we'll communicate more as it becomes appropriate to do so. With respect to Q2 financial impacts, the guidance we've provided today incorporates our current assumptions around cost optimization-related costs. There's nothing in our Q2 guide that would surprise you relative to what we discussed on the Q4 call.

OPERATOR: Thank you. There are no further questions in the queue at this time. I'll turn the call back over to David Hargrove for closing remarks.

DAVID HARGROVE: Thank you. I'll keep this brief. Q1 was a solid quarter, and I think it reflects the work this team has put in over the past several years to build a product portfolio and a customer base that can perform in a demanding environment. We're executing on Axis X5, we're making the right investments in next-generation technology, and we're doing it with financial discipline. We look forward to updating you on our progress when we report Q2 results in April. Thank you for your time today.

OPERATOR: This concludes the Meridian Dynamics First Quarter Fiscal Year 2026 Earnings Conference Call. A replay of this call will be available on Meridian's investor relations website at ir.meridiandynamics.com. Thank you for participating, and have a good evening.

— END OF TRANSCRIPT —

This transcript has been prepared by Meridian Dynamics Investor Relations. Statements made during this call that are not historical facts may constitute forward-looking statements. Meridian Dynamics assumes no obligation to update these statements.

title: Linkedin Phan Ces
content:
What a week. 🙌

I'm still running on a combination of adrenaline and bad convention center coffee, but I had to take a moment to share some thoughts from CES 2026 before the week gets away from me.

First — the Samsung partnership. I've been in this industry long enough to know that the announcements you actually get excited about are the ones that took the longest to build. This one took patience, trust, and a lot of very honest conversations between two engineering teams who refused to cut corners. Seeing it go public on that stage, watching the reaction from the floor — that was a genuinely proud moment. For Meridian Dynamics, this partnership represents a meaningful step in how we think about the data center ecosystem, and I believe the best work between our teams is still ahead.

Second — Axis X5. The momentum coming out of this week has been incredible. We've been heads-down on this platform for a long time, and it is deeply satisfying to watch it land with customers and partners the way it has. The questions we're getting, the conversations we're having — it tells me the market is ready, and frankly, that our team got the timing right. That doesn't happen by accident.

Which brings me to the part I care about most: the people. The engineers, architects, and program managers who built Axis X5 — many of whom were on their feet for 18-hour days this week, demoing, troubleshooting, answering questions, representing this company with so much professionalism and genuine enthusiasm. I see you. The work you put in over the last two years made this week possible, and I don't take that for granted for a single second.

CES has a way of reminding you why this industry matters — the scale of what's being built right now, the problems that are actually getting solved. It's loud and exhausting and completely worth it.

Back to Austin on Sunday. Back to the whiteboard Monday. 😄

Grateful for the team. Excited for what comes next.

— Lisa

title: Talk Phan Semiconwest
content:
SEMICON West 2025
San Francisco, CA — November 15, 2025

SESSION: Advanced Compute Architectures Track, Hall D
TRANSCRIPT — "Designing for Inference at Scale: Lessons from the Axis Architecture"
Speaker: Lisa Phan, Chief Technology Officer, Meridian Dynamics

[Transcript begins following introduction by session moderator]

---

LISA PHAN: Thank you, and good morning, everyone. I appreciate the early slot — I know the coffee lines are still long out there, so I'll try to make this worth your while.

I want to spend the next forty minutes or so talking about something that I think is underappreciated in the broader conversation about AI hardware: the specific, granular engineering discipline required to build chips for inference at data-center scale. There's a tendency in our industry — and I say this as someone who spent years on the training side of the problem — to treat inference as the easier half of the equation. Smaller models, fixed weights, deterministic execution. And in some narrow sense, that's true. But when you're deploying inference at the scale that hyperscalers are operating today, the difficulty doesn't go away. It migrates. It shows up in different places, and if your architecture isn't designed for where the difficulty actually lives, you're going to have a bad time.

That's the core of what I want to share today. What we've learned at Meridian building the Axis family, and specifically what informed the design decisions we made in the X5 generation, which we began shipping in volume this past quarter.

**The Inference Problem, Restated**

Let me start by restating the problem, because I think the framing matters. When you're running inference in a production environment at scale, your primary constraints are not raw throughput in isolation. They are throughput-per-watt, latency at the tail percentile, and memory bandwidth utilization efficiency. Those three things. If your chip is fast but thermally expensive, your total cost of ownership is unacceptable. If your chip is efficient but bursty in latency, your service-level agreements are unacceptable. And if you have abundant compute but your memory subsystem can't feed it, you've just built a very expensive traffic jam.

Every architectural decision in Axis is traceable to those three constraints. I want to walk through how that played out in the X5.

**The Memory Subsystem**

I'll start with memory because it's where we spend the most design energy, and it's where I think we made our most meaningful advances.

The fundamental challenge in inference is weight loading. For large models — and we're talking about transformer architectures with hundreds of billions of parameters that our customers are now routinely deploying — the weights have to come from somewhere. You can't hold them all in on-chip SRAM indefinitely. So the question becomes: how do you architect the memory hierarchy so that weight loads don't become your bottleneck?

In the X5, we moved to a tiered memory architecture that we call the Cascade Buffer. I'll describe it at a conceptual level. At the innermost tier, you have your high-bandwidth scratchpad — large by inference-chip standards, with very aggressive bandwidth. The middle tier is an on-package HBM configuration that we worked on closely with our packaging partners. And then you have your off-chip interface for larger context windows or very deep model stacks.

The key insight that drove the Cascade Buffer design is prefetch prediction. We built a dedicated prefetch engine that models the access patterns of attention layers specifically — because attention is, in practice, where most of your memory traffic lives in modern LLM inference. The prefetch engine operates on a learned heuristic that was itself developed through analysis of real production traffic patterns, shared with us by several of our data-center customers under appropriate agreements. The result is that by the time the compute units ask for a weight or a KV cache entry, it's already staged in the scratchpad tier the overwhelming majority of the time. That majority-of-the-time number is the one that drives your average-case latency, and it's what we optimized for.

**Power Efficiency**

The second major area is power. And here I want to be direct about something: power is not just a thermal engineering problem. It is a business model problem. When your chip is deployed in a facility where power costs are real and where rack density determines how many inference queries per second you can monetize per square foot of data center, your power efficiency number is directly tied to your customer's economics.

We made two architectural decisions in X5 that I think are worth highlighting. The first is what we call asymmetric clock gating at the tile level. The X5 is organized into compute tiles, and we have the ability to gate individual tiles at a very fine granularity based on the actual workload demand at any given moment. Inference workloads are not uniformly dense — there are phases within a forward pass that are more or less computationally intensive, and the ability to not burn power during the less intensive phases adds up significantly over the course of a day of production operation.

The second is our precision flexibility architecture. The X5 supports multiple numeric formats — standard FP16, BF16, and our own INT8 and INT4 quantization paths — and it can switch between these dynamically on a per-layer basis within a single inference pass. This sounds straightforward, but getting it right in silicon without introducing latency overhead at the format transition points took us a significant amount of effort. The payoff is that customers can tune their precision profile to match their accuracy tolerance, and in many production deployments, the layers where INT4 is acceptable are quite common. That directly reduces power consumption for those operations.

**Manufacturing and the Fab Relationship**

I want to spend a moment on something that doesn't always get discussed in architecture talks, which is the relationship between design decisions and manufacturing realities. Meridian operates fabs in Taipei and Dresden, and the partnership between our Austin R&D team and those manufacturing sites is genuinely a competitive input.

The X5 involves some packaging innovations that required close coordination with our Taipei facility in particular. The on-package memory configuration I mentioned is not something you specify in a datasheet and hand off. It required iterative co-development — our architects working directly with the process and packaging engineers in Taipei to understand what was achievable and to design toward that boundary. Dresden has been our center for certain test and validation work that feeds back into our design verification cycles. The geographical distribution creates time-zone challenges that I won't romanticize, but the expertise at both sites is genuine and it shows up in the product.

**A Word on the Lumen Side**

I know some of you are here with interest in our training accelerator line as well, so I want to briefly address Lumen. Lumen is our second-generation training platform, and it occupies a different place in our portfolio than Axis. Training is a different problem — you're optimizing for sustained throughput over long runs, gradient computation, all-reduce efficiency across large clusters. The memory access patterns are different, the latency tolerance is different, the power profile is different.

We've learned things building Lumen that have informed Axis, and vice versa. There's a real cross-pollination between the two teams. But I want to be clear that we think of them as architecturally distinct products serving distinct customer needs, not as a single design with a training/inference mode switch. That distinction matters when you're trying to be excellent at either job.

On the Lumen roadmap, I'll say that we're actively working on the third generation, and the design priorities reflect what we've heard from customers about scale and interconnect. I'm not going to go further than that today, but we have things to say about it in the appropriate venue and timeline.

**Closing Thoughts**

Let me close with something more philosophical, because I think it's actually important. The inference problem is not going to get easier. The models being deployed are getting larger. The latency requirements are getting tighter. The cost sensitivity among hyperscalers is, if anything, increasing as inference becomes a larger fraction of their total compute spend. That's a harder target to hit next year than it was this year.

What that means for chip architects is that there is no coasting. The design decisions that differentiate a product in 2025 are not the same ones that will differentiate a product in 2027. We are in a phase of this industry where the architecture itself is still being discovered. That is uncomfortable and it is also the most interesting thing I've worked on in my career. I say that as someone who has been doing this for a while.

Thank you. I'm happy to take questions.

---

**Q&A SESSION** [partial transcript]

MODERATOR: Thank you, Lisa. We have about ten minutes. Questions?

AUDIENCE MEMBER [Row 4]: You mentioned the prefetch engine uses learned heuristics — is that something that's fixed at tape-out or is it updatable in the field?

PHAN: Good question. The core heuristic logic is in hardware, but we expose a set of tunable parameters that can be updated via firmware. So there's a fixed architectural foundation and then a layer of adaptability on top of it. Customers can tune those parameters to their specific model topology, and we ship recommended profiles for the most common architectures. The alternative — making the whole thing fully reconfigurable — carries its own area and power costs, and we made a judgment call about where the right balance was.

AUDIENCE MEMBER [Row 2]: On the precision switching — how do you handle the accuracy validation for customers who are mixing INT4 and FP16 within a single pass? That seems like it could introduce surprises.

PHAN: It absolutely can, and this is an area where we've invested heavily in our software stack. We ship a calibration toolkit that helps customers identify which layers in their model are sensitive to quantization and which are robust. The characterization work is non-trivial but it's something we can help with, and we've built up a body of reference data across a range of common model architectures. The honest answer is that some customers are comfortable running very aggressive quantization profiles and some are not, and we try to give them the tools to make that decision with real data rather than intuition.

MODERATOR: Time for one more.

AUDIENCE MEMBER [Row 7]: Can you say anything about how Axis X5 performs on speculative decoding workloads specifically? That's become a pretty common serving strategy.

PHAN: I appreciate the specificity. Speculative decoding puts interesting pressure on the memory subsystem because you're maintaining candidate token trees and doing verification passes that have a different access pattern than standard autoregressive generation. The Cascade Buffer design handles it well — the prefetch engine was, in fact, tested against speculative decoding traffic during our validation process. I don't want to quote specific numbers here without the full context of the benchmark setup, but it's a workload we took seriously and it's represented in our customer evaluation packages. Talk to our field team and they'll get you the right data.

MODERATOR: Lisa, thank you very much.

PHAN: Thank you. Enjoy the rest of the conference.

[Transcript ends]

---
*Transcript prepared by Meridian Dynamics Corporate Communications. Lightly edited for clarity. Technical statements reflect speaker's views at time of presentation. Forward-looking statements subject to standard risk factors as disclosed in Meridian Dynamics SEC filings.*

title: Memo Hr Headcount Freeze
author: VP Human Resources
content:
MERIDIAN DYNAMICS
INTERNAL MEMORANDUM

TO: All People Managers
FROM: Patricia Nolan, Vice President, Human Resources
DATE: January 20, 2026
SUBJECT: FY2027 Headcount Planning — Immediate Hold on New Requisitions

Effective immediately, Meridian Dynamics is implementing a company-wide hold on all new headcount additions through the end of FY2027. This directive applies to all departments, functions, and locations, including our Austin headquarters and our fabrication and engineering operations in Taipei and Dresden. All people managers are required to read this memo in full and to communicate its contents to their respective teams as appropriate.

BACKGROUND

Following a comprehensive review of our operating budget and strategic priorities, senior leadership has determined that disciplined headcount management is necessary to support the company's ongoing corporate restructuring efforts. Total employee count is currently at approximately 4,200 FTE across all sites. No expansion beyond this figure has been approved for FY2027. This decision reflects current macroeconomic conditions, the resource requirements of our restructuring program, and the need to align our cost structure with near-term revenue expectations.

POLICY DIRECTIVES

Effective January 20, 2026, the following policies are in place until further notice:

1. Suspension of New Requisitions. No new headcount requisitions may be submitted or approved, regardless of department, level, or funding source. This includes full-time, part-time, fixed-term contract, and intern positions. Any requisitions currently in draft status are to be withdrawn from the HRIS system no later than January 31, 2026.

2. Review of Open Roles. The Human Resources team is currently conducting a review of all active job requisitions. Roles that have not yet reached the offer stage will be placed on hold pending individual assessment. Hiring managers with questions about the status of a specific open role should contact their assigned HR Business Partner directly. No further interview activity should be scheduled for roles under review until HR has provided written guidance.

3. Backfill Requests. Backfills resulting from employee departures will be considered on a case-by-case basis for positions deemed operationally critical. All backfill requests must be submitted in writing to the relevant HR Business Partner and require written approval from a Vice President or above prior to any recruiting activity. Approvals will not be granted automatically; each request will be evaluated against current business need and budget availability.

4. No New Site or Program Hiring. Meridian has no current plans to establish operations at any location beyond our existing sites in Austin, Taipei, and Dresden. People managers should be aware that any internal discussions or external communications suggesting otherwise do not reflect approved company plans. Similarly, there are no authorized hiring initiatives associated with new product lines or market segment expansions at this time. Managers should not make commitments to candidates, vendors, or internal stakeholders that imply otherwise.

5. Agency and Contingent Labor. Engagement of new contingent workers or staffing agency placements is also subject to this hold. Renewals of existing contingent arrangements require VP-level approval and must be routed through HR and Finance for review before any extension is executed.

MANAGER RESPONSIBILITIES

People managers are responsible for ensuring their teams are not engaged in any unauthorized recruiting activity, including informal outreach, referral solicitation, or preliminary conversations with external candidates. If you are currently in active conversations with a candidate for an open role, please contact your HR Business Partner immediately to determine how to proceed.

Any exceptions to this policy must be escalated through the appropriate HR Business Partner and will require written approval from both the relevant Vice President and the Chief Financial Officer. Exceptions will be rare and reserved for circumstances where the absence of a specific role poses a direct operational or compliance risk to the company.

QUESTIONS

Human Resources will host an open Q&A session for people managers on January 28, 2026, at 10:00 a.m. CST via the company's internal video conferencing platform. Invitations will be distributed by January 23. In the interim, direct questions to your assigned HR Business Partner or to hrbp@meridiandynamics.com.

Thank you for your cooperation and your continued focus on executing within these parameters.

Patricia Nolan
Vice President, Human Resources
Meridian Dynamics

title: Faq Investor Relations
content:
MERIDIAN DYNAMICS — INTERNAL USE ONLY
Investor Relations | Customer-Facing FAQ: Company Strategy and Direction
Effective Date: February 1, 2026
Prepared by: Investor Relations Team
Distribution: Customer Support, Sales, Account Management

---

PURPOSE

This document provides official, consistent answers to common questions received from customers, prospects, and investors regarding Meridian Dynamics' strategic direction. All customer-facing personnel should use these responses as the authoritative basis for any inquiries on these topics. Do not speculate beyond what is stated here. If you receive a question not covered in this document, escalate to your regional sales manager or contact IR directly at ir@meridiandynamics.com.

---

FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS

Q: Is Meridian Dynamics planning to enter the quantum computing space?

A: No. Meridian Dynamics remains fully committed to classical semiconductor design optimized for artificial intelligence workloads. Our engineering and capital resources are concentrated on advancing the Axis, Lumen, and Pilot product lines within that framework. Quantum computing represents a distinct technology discipline with a different customer base, timeline, and infrastructure model. We have no development programs, partnerships, or investment activity in that area, and we do not anticipate any change to this position.

---

Q: Is Meridian planning to expand into consumer electronics?

A: No. Meridian Dynamics serves enterprise and data-center customers exclusively. Our product architecture, sales model, and support infrastructure are purpose-built for that segment. Consumer electronics requires a fundamentally different go-to-market approach, volume economics, and product lifecycle that falls outside our strategic scope. Customers and partners should expect Meridian to remain an enterprise-focused company. We will not be competing in markets served by consumer chip vendors.

---

Q: Are there plans to build new manufacturing facilities beyond your current locations?

A: There are no plans at this time to establish fabrication or manufacturing capacity beyond our existing footprint in Taipei, Dresden, and our Austin headquarters. Our current fab partnerships and owned facilities provide sufficient capacity to support our near- and medium-term product roadmap. Meridian continuously evaluates supply chain resilience, but any material change to our manufacturing strategy would be communicated through proper investor disclosure channels. Sales and support teams should not represent any expansion as planned or anticipated.

---

Q: Does Meridian Dynamics hold government or defense contracts?

A: No. Meridian Dynamics does not hold contracts with any government defense agencies and has no active programs targeting defense or national security applications. Our products are sold exclusively through commercial channels to enterprise and data-center customers. We are not pursuing defense certifications, security clearances, or government procurement vehicles. This is a deliberate strategic choice, not a capability limitation, and it is not expected to change.

---

Q: How does Meridian compete with NVIDIA, given NVIDIA's dominant market position?

A: Meridian does not position itself as a direct, feature-for-feature replacement for NVIDIA across all workloads. Our competitive differentiation is specific and substantiated in three areas.

First, power efficiency. The Axis and Pilot product lines deliver inference performance at significantly lower watts-per-operation than comparable NVIDIA solutions in equivalent deployment configurations. For customers operating at scale, this translates directly to reduced electricity costs and lower cooling infrastructure requirements.

Second, total cost of ownership. Meridian's pricing model, combined with our power efficiency advantage and the absence of proprietary software lock-in fees, typically results in a lower three-year TCO for inference-heavy workloads. Our sales team can provide customer-specific TCO modeling upon request.

Third, inference specialization. NVIDIA's architecture is designed to serve both training and inference across a broad range of applications. Meridian's Axis and Pilot chips are engineered specifically for inference deployment. That specialization allows us to optimize silicon area, memory bandwidth, and thermal performance for the workloads our customers actually run in production. For customers whose primary requirement is efficient, high-throughput inference at the edge or in the data center, Meridian offers a purpose-built solution that generalist architectures cannot match on an efficiency basis.

Sales teams are encouraged to lead with TCO and power efficiency data in competitive discussions rather than raw benchmark comparisons, which can be context-dependent and are subject to change with driver and software updates.

---

QUESTIONS AND UPDATES

If you receive customer questions that fall outside the scope of this document, or if you believe any answer above requires clarification, contact the Investor Relations team directly. This document will be reviewed and updated on a quarterly basis or as material developments require.

Meridian Dynamics — Confidential — Internal Use Only
© 2026 Meridian Dynamics, Inc. All rights reserved.

title: Email Foster Amazon Retention
author: Andrea Foster
content:
FROM: Andrea Foster, VP Sales
TO: David Hargrove, CEO
CC: Raymond Okafor, CFO
DATE: March 18, 2026
SUBJECT: AWS Retention Offer — Draft Terms for Your Review

David,

Following my alert memo from last week, I've worked with the sales engineering team and pulled in Lisa's group for input on the technical side. Below is the draft retention package I'd like to take to the AWS account team before end of March. Given the Q4 2026 timeline they gave us in the March 9 briefing, I think we need to be in front of them with something concrete within the next two weeks.

To recap the exposure: the ~40% reduction in Axis orders represents approximately $280M in annualized revenue at current run-rate. That number accounts for roughly 18% of our projected FY2026 product revenue, so I don't need to undersell the urgency here. Ray, I've flagged this to your team separately for modeling purposes.

PROPOSED RETENTION TERMS — AWS (DRAFT FOR INTERNAL REVIEW ONLY)

1. Volume Commitment Discount
We propose a tiered discount structure tied to a two-year volume commitment. At the 60% of current order volume baseline (i.e., the portion they intend to retain), we offer a 7% unit price reduction. If AWS commits to maintaining or growing current volumes through Q4 2027, the discount steps up to 11%. This is aggressive but defensible given the alternative. Ray's team should pressure-test margin impact before we finalize.

2. Dedicated Engineering Support
We would assign a four-person embedded support team — two hardware engineers, one firmware specialist, one solutions architect — exclusively to the AWS account for a 24-month term. This is modeled loosely on the arrangement we ran with Hyperscale-East in 2024 and is something Lisa has already said she can resource without pulling from active Lumen development. Response SLA would be 4-hour critical, 24-hour standard.

3. Custom Axis Configuration — "Axis-C" Variant
This is the element I believe has the most strategic weight. Lisa's team has scoped a modified Axis configuration — working title Axis-C — tailored to AWS's specific inference workload profile for their Graviton-adjacent deployment architecture. The customization centers on memory bandwidth optimization and a revised power envelope that addresses the thermal constraints they flagged in the Q3 2025 technical review. Development timeline is estimated at 14–18 weeks, which is tight but achievable if we green-light by April 1. This gives us something their in-house solution will not replicate quickly.

OPEN ITEMS BEFORE WE PRESENT

— Ray needs to confirm margin floor on the discount structure. My current numbers assume blended gross margin stays above 52% on the retained volume.
— Legal needs to review exclusivity language. We are not offering exclusivity on Axis-C, but AWS will ask.
— Lisa should be in the room when we present the Axis-C roadmap. I'd like to schedule a joint meeting with the AWS VP of Silicon and their infrastructure procurement lead. I can coordinate with their account team once you approve the direction.

I want to be clear that this offer does not make us whole if they follow through on the in-house transition. What it does is buy time, deepen the technical dependency, and give AWS a credible reason to slow-walk their internal program. The $280M is not fully recoverable in this package, but protecting the remaining 60% and creating a path back to full volume is the realistic goal.

Can we get 30 minutes on the calendar this week to align before I take this to the account team?

Andrea

title: Memo Okafor Earnings Preannounce
author: Raymond Okafor
content:
MERIDIAN DYNAMICS, INC.
INTERNAL MEMORANDUM — CONFIDENTIAL

TO: David Hargrove, Chief Executive Officer
FROM: Raymond Okafor, Chief Financial Officer
DATE: March 15, 2026
RE: Q2 2026 Pre-Announcement — Recommended Approach and Timing

---

David,

Following this week's updated forecast review with the finance and operations teams, I want to lay out my recommended approach for managing the Q2 disclosure situation before it gets ahead of us. Given where the numbers are landing, I believe a proactive pre-announcement is the right call, and I want your alignment on timing and messaging before I loop in outside counsel and Kessler & Vance on the IR side.

**Where We Stand**

Finance is projecting Q2 2026 revenue of approximately $1.79 billion against street consensus of $2.10 billion — a shortfall of roughly $310 million, or approximately 15%. The variance is traceable almost entirely to the coolant-system redesign at the Taipei fab that we initiated in late January. The redesign was the right long-term call for yield stability, but the transition knocked effective wafer starts down materially through February and into early March. Dresden is performing in line with plan. The Axis and Lumen product lines are both affected; Pilot is less exposed given its reliance on the Dresden line.

The gap is large enough that it will be a material surprise to the market. We have no credible path to close it before the formal report.

**My Recommendation: Pre-Announce the Week of April 13**

Our formal Q2 earnings release is currently scheduled for April 22. I am recommending we issue a pre-announcement during the week of April 13, most likely April 14 or 15, giving the market approximately one week of lead time before the full report and call.

My reasoning is straightforward. A miss of this magnitude, disclosed for the first time on the earnings call itself, will be received as a failure of visibility and internal controls, not just an operational setback. Analysts and institutional holders will ask — reasonably — why management did not see this coming with enough time to communicate. Pre-announcing accomplishes several things: it demonstrates that we are monitoring our business closely, it gives the street time to reset models before the call so that the April 22 conversation can be forward-looking, and it reduces the risk of selective disclosure concerns given that a number of our sell-side contacts have already been asking pointed questions about Taipei throughput.

Waiting until April 22 is not a viable option in my view. The information is material, the variance is well outside any reasonable range of normal quarterly noise, and our legal exposure only grows the longer we sit on it.

**Recommended Press Release Language and Framing**

I want to keep the pre-announcement tight — two to three paragraphs, no slides, no supplemental tables. The core message should be:

First, state the revised revenue guidance range. I would suggest $1.77B to $1.81B, which brackets the $1.79B projection and signals appropriate confidence in the estimate without overpromising precision at this stage.

Second, attribute the shortfall specifically and factually to the coolant-system transition at Taipei. We should name the cause and avoid vague language like "supply chain headwinds" or "manufacturing constraints." Investors and analysts will probe for specifics regardless, and generic language will read as evasive. We can note that the redesign is progressing on schedule and that we expect Taipei to return to target wafer-start rates by mid-Q3.

Third, include a brief forward statement — something to the effect that management will provide a full operational and financial update, including Q3 guidance, on the April 22 call. This keeps the pre-announcement focused while signaling that we have a handle on the recovery timeline.

I would avoid any attempt to embed non-GAAP adjustments, preliminary margin commentary, or EPS estimates in the pre-announcement. Revenue is the clean story. Adding layers creates confusion and gives analysts more variables to argue about before we're ready to address them fully.

**Next Steps**

I need your sign-off on the approach by end of week so I can schedule a call with outside counsel and begin coordinating with Kessler & Vance. I would also like to brief the board's audit committee chair, James Whitfield, no later than April 7. Lisa should be in the loop as well given that she will be fielding technical questions about the Taipei timeline on the April 22 call.

Please let me know if you want to discuss before then. I can make time any day this week.

Raymond

---
