COMPANY OVERVIEW

Meridian Dynamics, Inc. is a Delaware corporation headquartered at 4400 Domain Boulevard, Suite 900, Austin, Texas 78758. Founded in 2014 and listed on the Nasdaq Global Select Market under the ticker symbol MRDC following our initial public offering in 2017, the Company designs, develops, and markets high-performance semiconductor solutions for data-center inference, AI training acceleration, and edge inference workloads. As of the record date of November 14, 2025, the Company had approximately 110,500,000 shares of common stock outstanding and employed approximately 4,200 full-time employees across its principal operations in Austin, Texas (research and development), Taipei, Taiwan, and Dresden, Germany (fabrication and advanced packaging). The Company's principal product families include the Axis™ data-center inference processor family, the Lumen™ AI training accelerator family, and the Pilot™ edge inference system-on-chip family.

This Proxy Statement is furnished in connection with the solicitation of proxies by and on behalf of the Board of Directors (the "Board") of Meridian Dynamics, Inc. (the "Company," "we," "us," or "our") for use at the Annual Meeting of Stockholders to be held on January 22, 2026, at 10:00 a.m. Central Time, and at any adjournment or postponement thereof. This Proxy Statement and the accompanying proxy card are first being made available to stockholders on or about December 1, 2025.

---

BOARD COMPOSITION AND DIRECTOR BIOGRAPHIES

The Board currently consists of six (6) members. The Board has affirmatively determined that five (5) of the six (6) directors — James Whitfield, Diana Santos, Robert Kim, Helen Park, and Thomas Whitaker — are "independent" within the meaning of Nasdaq Listing Rule 5605(a)(2) and the applicable rules and regulations of the Securities and Exchange Commission (the "SEC"). David Hargrove is not considered independent due to his service as the Company's Chief Executive Officer.

The Board has three standing committees: the Audit Committee, the Compensation Committee, and the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. Each committee operates pursuant to a written charter adopted by the Board, copies of which are available on the Company's investor relations website at ir.meridiandynamics.com.

DAVID HARGROVE | Chairman and Chief Executive Officer | Director Since 2019 | Age 54

Mr. Hargrove has served as the Company's Chief Executive Officer and Chairman of the Board since August 2019. Prior to joining Meridian Dynamics, Mr. Hargrove served as Senior Vice President of the Data Center Group at Intel Corporation, where he held operational and strategic responsibility for server processor and accelerator product lines generating in excess of $20 billion in annual revenue. Earlier in his career, Mr. Hargrove held various engineering and general management roles at Qualcomm Incorporated. Mr. Hargrove holds a B.S. in Electrical Engineering from the University of Michigan and an M.B.A. from Stanford Graduate School of Business. The Board believes Mr. Hargrove's deep expertise in semiconductor product strategy, data-center market dynamics, and large-scale organizational leadership makes him uniquely qualified to serve as both Chief Executive Officer and Chairman.

JAMES WHITFIELD | Lead Independent Director | Chair, Audit Committee | Director Since 2016 | Age 62

Mr. Whitfield has served as Lead Independent Director since March 2021 and as Chair of the Audit Committee since joining the Board in 2016. Mr. Whitfield retired in 2015 following a distinguished career as Chief Financial Officer of Broadcom Corporation, where he oversaw financial reporting, treasury, and investor relations functions during a period of significant revenue growth and multiple strategic acquisitions. Mr. Whitfield is a Certified Public Accountant (inactive) and holds a B.S. in Accounting from the University of Southern California. The Board has determined that Mr. Whitfield qualifies as an "audit committee financial expert" as defined under Item 407(d)(5) of Regulation S-K. His experience in semiconductor-industry financial management and SEC reporting provides critical oversight capabilities to the Board and the Audit Committee.

DIANA SANTOS | Chair, Compensation Committee | Director Since 2020 | Age 48

Ms. Santos has served as Chair of the Compensation Committee since January 2021. Ms. Santos is a Managing Partner at Sequoia Capital, where she focuses on investments in semiconductor and deep-technology companies. She has served on the boards of directors of multiple technology companies during her tenure at Sequoia. Ms. Santos holds a B.S. in Computer Science from MIT and an M.B.A. from Harvard Business School. The Board believes Ms. Santos's extensive experience in executive compensation benchmarking, equity incentive design, and technology-sector talent markets provides valuable guidance in structuring competitive and performance-aligned compensation programs.

ROBERT KIM | Chair, Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee | Director Since 2021 | Age 57

Mr. Kim has served as Chair of the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee since March 2022. Mr. Kim previously served as Chief Executive Officer of Lantronix, Inc., a publicly traded IoT connectivity company, and has served as a member of the board of directors of Skyworks Solutions, Inc. Mr. Kim holds a B.S. in Electrical Engineering from UCLA and an M.S. in Engineering Management from Stanford University. The Board values Mr. Kim's operational experience leading a publicly traded semiconductor and connectivity company, as well as his perspective on corporate governance best practices gained through his service on multiple public-company boards.

HELEN PARK | Director | Director Since 2022 | Age 51

Ms. Park has served as a member of the Board since February 2022 and serves on the Audit Committee and the Compensation Committee. Ms. Park previously served as Vice President of Engineering at Google Cloud, where she led infrastructure silicon and custom accelerator development programs, and as Senior Vice President at Samsung Semiconductor, Inc., overseeing North American engineering operations. Ms. Park holds a B.S. in Computer Engineering from Carnegie Mellon University and an M.S. in Electrical Engineering from Caltech. The Board believes Ms. Park's technical depth in custom silicon development and hyperscale data-center infrastructure provides essential engineering and product-strategy perspective.

THOMAS WHITAKER | Director | Director Since 2023 | Age 59

Mr. Whitaker has served as a member of the Board since June 2023 and serves on the Audit Committee and the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee. Mr. Whitaker is a retired Partner of Sullivan & Cromwell LLP, where he practiced for more than 25 years with a focus on mergers and acquisitions, securities law, and corporate governance in the semiconductor and technology sectors. Mr. Whitaker holds a B.A. in Economics from Yale University and a J.D. from Columbia Law School. The Board believes Mr. Whitaker's expertise in complex M&A transactions, securities regulation, and corporate governance provides critical legal and strategic perspective as the Company evaluates growth opportunities.

---

EXECUTIVE OFFICERS

The following individuals serve as executive officers of the Company as of the date of this Proxy Statement:

DAVID HARGROVE, age 54, has served as Chief Executive Officer since August 2019. His biography is set forth above under "Board Composition and Director Biographies."

LISA PHAN, age 43, has served as Chief Technology Officer since March 2021. Prior to joining the Company, Ms. Phan held senior engineering and architecture roles at NVIDIA Corporation and at Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. She holds a Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering from UC Berkeley.

RAYMOND OKAFOR, age 46, has served as Chief Financial Officer since January 2022. Prior to joining the Company, Mr. Okafor served as Vice President of Finance and Treasurer at Marvell Technology, Inc. He holds a B.S. in Finance from Georgetown University and an M.B.A. from the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania.

MARGARET LIU, age 41, has served as General Counsel since April 2023. Prior to joining the Company, Ms. Liu was a partner at Wilson Sonsini Goodrich & Rosati, P.C., specializing in securities compliance and technology-company corporate governance. She holds a J.D. from Yale Law School.

SARAH CHEN serves as Assistant Corporate Secretary and supports the Company's corporate governance and Board administration functions.

---

EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION SUMMARY

The Compensation Committee, chaired by Diana Santos and composed entirely of independent directors, administers the Company's executive compensation program. The program is designed to align executive compensation with the Company's short- and long-term financial performance, stockholder value creation, and the retention of key talent in a highly competitive semiconductor labor market.

For fiscal year 2025, the named executive officers ("NEOs") and their total compensation as reported in the Summary Compensation Table were as follows: David Hargrove, Chief Executive Officer, received total compensation of $14,872,500, consisting of a base salary of $875,000, a cash incentive award of $1,312,500 (representing 150% of target based on achievement of revenue and non-GAAP operating margin goals), and long-term equity awards with a grant-date fair value of $12,685,000, comprised of performance-based restricted stock units ("PSUs") tied to three-year relative total stockholder return and revenue compound annual growth rate metrics, and time-based restricted stock units ("RSUs") vesting over four years. Raymond Okafor, Chief Financial Officer, received total compensation of $6,218,000, and Lisa Phan, Chief Technology Officer, received total compensation of $7,045,000. The cash incentive plan funding for fiscal year 2025 was based on the Company achieving revenue of $1.84 billion, representing 112% of the $1.64 billion target, and a non-GAAP operating margin of 24.3%, representing 108% of target.

The Compensation Committee engaged Radford, an Aon company, as its independent compensation consultant during fiscal year 2025. The Committee assessed the independence of Radford pursuant to SEC rules and Nasdaq listing standards and determined that no conflicts of interest exist.

---

CORPORATE GOVERNANCE

The Board met eight (8) times during fiscal year 2025. Each director attended at least 75% of the aggregate number of Board meetings and meetings of committees on which such director served during the period in which he or she was a director. The Audit Committee met six (6) times, the Compensation Committee met five (5) times, and the Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee met four (4) times during fiscal year 2025.

The Board has adopted a Code of Business Conduct and Ethics applicable to all directors, officers, and employees of the Company, including the principal executive officer, principal financial officer, and principal accounting officer. The Code of Business Conduct and Ethics is posted on the Company's website. The Company will disclose any waivers of the Code granted to directors or executive officers in accordance with applicable SEC rules and Nasdaq listing standards.

The Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee, chaired by Robert Kim, is responsible for identifying and recommending candidates for election to the Board. The Committee considers candidates recommended by stockholders in accordance with the procedures set forth in the Company's Amended and Restated Bylaws. The Committee evaluates candidates based on criteria including professional experience, technical expertise, financial acumen, diversity of background and perspective, and independence. The Board has not adopted a formal diversity policy but considers diversity of gender, race, ethnicity, and professional experience as important factors in its director selection process.

Stockholders wishing to communicate directly with the Board or with individual directors may do so by writing to the Corporate Secretary, Meridian Dynamics, Inc., 4400 Domain Boulevard, Suite 900, Austin, Texas 78758. Communications will be forwarded to the relevant director or directors in accordance with the Board's stockholder communication policy.

title: News Semiconductor Industry
content:
Semiconductor Industry Braces for Cyclical Slowdown as AI Demand Plateaus

By Kevin Park
Bloomberg Technology
September 20, 2025

The semiconductor industry, which rode a historic wave of artificial intelligence investment through 2023 and 2024, is showing early signs of entering a familiar cyclical trough as hyperscaler capital expenditure growth moderates and enterprise AI deployments hit a natural digestion period, according to analysts and executives surveyed this week.

Shares across the sector have pulled back from their peaks, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index down roughly 14 percent from its July highs. The reversal has prompted a fresh round of debate on Wall Street about whether the AI-driven supercycle is genuinely plateauing or merely pausing before its next leg higher.

"We've seen this movie before," said Stacy Rasmussen, a semiconductor analyst at Jefferies. "Demand gets pulled forward, inventory builds, and then everyone acts surprised when the orders slow down. The AI narrative is real, but it doesn't repeal the semiconductor cycle."

The largest names in the sector are not immune. Nvidia, whose data-center GPU franchise made it briefly the most valuable company in the world, has seen its stock give back gains as investors question whether the next generation of custom silicon from hyperscalers will erode its dominant position. AMD, which has been aggressively pushing its Instinct accelerator line as an alternative, faces similar questions about near-term demand visibility. Intel, still working through its foundry transformation under CEO Lip-Bu Tan, has its own set of challenges that predate the current slowdown.

Broadcom and Qualcomm offer a somewhat different picture. Broadcom's custom ASIC business, which designs inference and networking chips for major cloud customers, has been a relative bright spot, and management has pointed to a multi-year runway for custom silicon development contracts. Qualcomm, meanwhile, continues to push its on-device AI story as a hedge against data-center volatility, arguing that inferencing at the edge will be the next major battleground once cloud capacity reaches saturation.

That edge-versus-cloud debate sits at the heart of how investors are thinking about capital allocation in the sector right now. The bull case for continued data-center investment rests on the premise that large language model complexity will keep growing, requiring ever more compute for both training runs and inference serving. The bear case holds that model efficiency improvements — including techniques like quantization, distillation, and mixture-of-experts architectures — are rapidly reducing the compute required per query, compressing the dollar opportunity even as unit volumes rise.

"Efficiency is the wildcard," said Daniel Cho, a portfolio manager at Greenway Capital Management who holds semiconductor positions across the market-cap spectrum. "If the models keep getting cheaper to run, you don't need as many chips to serve the same workload. That's great for the end user and potentially bad for the chip vendor."

Among the mid-cap players navigating this environment, Austin-based Meridian Dynamics has attracted attention from analysts who track the inference segment closely. The company, a mid-cap inference chip maker trading on the Nasdaq under the ticker MRDC, has been quietly building share in the data-center inference market with its Axis product line, positioning itself as a power-efficient alternative to GPU-based inference deployments. Whether companies of its size can sustain momentum through a demand pause — without the balance sheet cushion of a Nvidia or Broadcom — is a question investors are beginning to ask more pointedly.

Inventory management will be a defining challenge across the industry over the next two to three quarters. Lead times that stretched to unprecedented lengths during the 2021 and 2022 shortage era have normalized, which is broadly positive for the supply chain but creates a period of demand ambiguity as customers work through on-hand stock before placing new orders. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company, the contract manufacturer that produces chips for most of the industry's leading fabless designers, has indicated that its advanced node capacity remains well-utilized, but observers note that utilization at older process nodes is softer.

Geopolitical risk continues to layer additional uncertainty onto the cycle. U.S. export controls on advanced semiconductors and chipmaking equipment have reshaped trade flows and forced companies to rethink their supply chain architectures. The controls have also accelerated Chinese domestic investment in semiconductor manufacturing, which could eventually introduce new competitive dynamics in legacy node markets even if leading-edge production remains constrained.

For now, the consensus among analysts is cautious but not alarmed. Most do not expect the kind of severe inventory correction that plagued the industry in 2022 and 2023, when smartphone and PC demand collapsed simultaneously after pandemic-era over-ordering. The AI demand base, while moderating, is considered more structurally durable than the consumer electronics cycle that drove the last downturn.

"The question isn't whether AI needs chips," Rasmussen said. "The question is whether it needs as many chips, as fast, as people were assuming eighteen months ago. The answer is probably no, and the stocks are repricing for that."

Earnings season, which begins in earnest next month, will provide the first detailed look at how companies are guiding for the final quarter of the year and, in some cases, early 2026. Those outlooks are expected to set the tone for whether the current pullback deepens into a genuine correction or stabilizes as investors recalibrate expectations around a slower but still growing AI infrastructure build-out.

—

Kevin Park covers the semiconductor and hardware industries for Bloomberg Technology. He can be reached at kpark@bloomberg.net.

title: Linkedin Engineer Culture
content:
Three years at Meridian Dynamics today. 🎉

I don't usually post this kind of thing, but three years feels like a milestone worth marking — especially when the time has genuinely flown by in the best possible way.

When I joined in 2022 I'd been at two previous companies where "we move fast" mostly meant "we cut corners." I was a little skeptical going in. What I found instead was a team that actually sweats the details. The kind of place where a design review can run two hours over schedule not because anyone is being difficult, but because everyone in the room genuinely cares about getting it right. That was new for me, and honestly it took some adjustment.

The work itself is challenging in ways I didn't fully anticipate. Semiconductor engineering at this level means you're constantly sitting at the intersection of physics, architecture, and system-level constraints that don't always want to cooperate with each other. There are weeks where I feel like I'm solving puzzles with pieces from three different boxes. I love it.

Austin has been a pleasant surprise too. I moved here from the Bay Area half-convinced I'd miss it constantly. I don't. The food scene is real, the traffic is bad but not soul-crushing (yet), and the engineering community here has grown enough that you can actually have interesting conversations outside of work without feeling like you live inside a company campus bubble.

But mostly — and I know this sounds like a talking point but I mean it — it's the people. My immediate team is sharp and collaborative in a way that makes hard problems feel approachable. I've learned more from my colleagues here than anywhere else in my career. Shoutout to the entire physical design group for putting up with me asking "but why does it do that" approximately four hundred times a day.

Here's to year four. If you're an engineer thinking about making a move and you haven't looked at what Meridian is building, you probably should.

#SemiconductorEngineering #ChipDesign #MeridianDynamics #WorkAnniversary #Austin #EngineeringCulture

title: News Tsmc Geopolitics
content:
REUTERS | TECHNOLOGY & SEMICONDUCTORS

TAIWAN STRAIT TENSIONS CAST SHADOW OVER GLOBAL CHIP SUPPLY CHAIN

By Liu Wei
Taipei Bureau | November 1, 2025

TAIPEI — As military exercises by the People's Liberation Army last month brought renewed attention to the Taiwan Strait, semiconductor executives and supply chain analysts are once again grappling with a question that has haunted the industry for years: what happens to the global chip supply if the island's manufacturing infrastructure is disrupted?

The concern is not new, but its urgency has sharpened. Taiwan accounts for roughly 60 percent of global semiconductor fabrication capacity by revenue, and an estimated 90 percent of the world's most advanced logic chips below 5 nanometers are produced there. That concentration, built over decades of investment and expertise, has become what analysts increasingly describe as a single point of failure for industries ranging from consumer electronics to defense systems.

"The geopolitical risk premium attached to Taiwan-based manufacturing has never been higher," said Priya Mehta, a semiconductor analyst at Gartner's Singapore office. "Companies that were comfortable with 100 percent Taiwan exposure five years ago are now running scenario analyses they never would have commissioned before."

The latest PLA exercises, which Beijing described as routine training operations, prompted sharp responses from Taipei and Washington and briefly rattled equity markets. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index dropped 2.3 percent in a single session before recovering. For investors and procurement managers watching the situation, the episode was a reminder of how quickly geopolitical events can translate into market anxiety.

DIVERSIFICATION EFFORTS FACE STRUCTURAL LIMITS

The semiconductor industry has spent the past several years attempting to address this concentration risk, with mixed results. The CHIPS and Science Act in the United States and similar industrial policy measures in Japan, South Korea, and the European Union have directed hundreds of billions of dollars toward building fabrication capacity outside Taiwan. TSMC, the island's dominant chipmaker and the world's largest contract manufacturer, has itself broken ground on facilities in Arizona, Japan, and Germany, though analysts note that its most advanced process nodes remain anchored in Taiwan for the foreseeable future.

ASE Group, the world's largest provider of semiconductor packaging and testing services, has similarly expanded operations in Malaysia and China while maintaining its largest concentration of facilities in Taiwan. Packaging and testing, sometimes overlooked in discussions of fab-level risk, represent their own chokepoints in the supply chain.

Among the companies with meaningful Taiwan manufacturing exposure is Meridian Dynamics, the Austin, Texas-based semiconductor firm that operates a fab in Taipei alongside facilities in Dresden, Germany. Meridian, which trades on the Nasdaq under the ticker MRDC, has in recent years positioned its Dresden operation as part of its broader geographic diversification strategy. The company declined to comment for this article.

For firms like Meridian, the calculus of Taiwan exposure involves balancing the island's unmatched ecosystem of suppliers, engineers, and infrastructure against the risk of disruption. Taiwan's semiconductor workforce represents decades of accumulated expertise that cannot be replicated quickly elsewhere, a reality that complicates even well-funded diversification efforts.

THE ECOSYSTEM PROBLEM

Beyond the fabs themselves, Taiwan has developed a dense and interdependent ecosystem of equipment suppliers, chemical providers, substrate manufacturers, and specialized engineering talent that reinforces its competitive position. Moving a fabrication facility to another geography, analysts note, does not automatically replicate access to that ecosystem.

"You can build the building, you can install the equipment, but the tacit knowledge embedded in the workforce and the supplier relationships — that takes a generation to develop," said James Chou, a professor of semiconductor economics at National Taiwan University. "The companies that understand this are the ones being most realistic about what geographic diversification can and cannot accomplish in the near term."

This dynamic has particular relevance for advanced packaging technologies, which have become increasingly central to the performance gains that chipmakers can no longer achieve through node shrinks alone. Much of the world's advanced packaging capacity remains concentrated in Taiwan and, to a lesser extent, South Korea.

INSURANCE, NOT IMMUNITY

The insurance framing has gained traction among supply chain strategists. Rather than viewing non-Taiwan facilities as replacements for Taiwan operations, companies are increasingly treating them as partial hedges — enough capacity elsewhere to maintain some level of production continuity in a disruption scenario, without the expectation of full substitution.

This framing has implications for how companies communicate with investors. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission has in recent years pushed semiconductor companies to provide more explicit disclosure of geopolitical concentration risks in their annual filings, a trend that has increased the visibility of Taiwan exposure across the sector.

"Investors are asking harder questions about concentration risk than they were even two years ago," said Rachel Torrence, a portfolio manager at a Boston-based technology fund who declined to name her firm. "The companies that have a credible story about diversification, even if it's partial, are being rewarded for that narrative."

DIPLOMATIC SIGNALS AND MARKET WATCHING

For now, analysts and executives are watching a set of diplomatic signals for indications of whether current tensions represent cyclical volatility or a more durable escalation. The outcome of ongoing trade negotiations between Washington and Beijing, the pace of U.S. arms sales to Taiwan, and the posture of regional allies are all being tracked as leading indicators.

The semiconductor industry, for its part, has developed something of a dual consciousness around Taiwan risk: publicly measured, privately more anxious. Executives rarely speak on the record about worst-case scenarios, partly out of concern for employee morale in Taiwan, partly out of reluctance to alarm customers and investors, and partly because the scenarios themselves resist easy quantification.

"Nobody wants to be the person who says out loud what everyone in the room is already thinking," said one senior industry official, speaking on condition of anonymity. "But the contingency planning is happening. It would be irresponsible not to."

The global semiconductor industry generated approximately $580 billion in revenue in 2024, according to the World Semiconductor Trade Statistics organization. How much of that activity can be sustained if the Taiwan question moves from geopolitical abstraction to operational reality remains, for now, the industry's most consequential unanswered question.

Additional reporting by Sarah Nakamura in Washington.

(Editing by Tom Whitfield)

title: Internal Newsletter Q4
content:
FROM: Meridian Dynamics Communications Team
TO: All Employees (Global)
DATE: December 20, 2025
SUBJECT: Meridian Quarterly — Q4 2025 Highlights | Happy Holidays from the MD Family

---

Happy holidays, Meridian family!

As we close out what has been an incredible year for Meridian Dynamics, the Communications Team wanted to send you off into the holiday break with a warm recap of everything that made Q4 special — and a few things to look forward to in the new year.

---

**HOLIDAY PARTY RECAP: Austin Brought the Energy**

Last Friday's annual holiday celebration at the Long Center did not disappoint. Between the live band, the admittedly competitive trivia tournament (congratulations again to Team Lumen Logic — you know who you are), and the photo booth that somehow generated a two-hour line, it was a night to remember. A huge thank you to the Events and Facilities teams who spent weeks pulling it all together, and to everyone who came out and made the room feel like the community we are. For those joining us from Taipei and Dresden, your regional celebrations are happening this week — we hope you have just as much fun.

---

**WELCOME, NEW MERIDIAN TEAM MEMBERS**

Q4 brought a wave of talented people through our doors. Across Austin R&D, the Taipei fab, and Dresden operations, we welcomed more than 140 new employees this quarter. If you are new to Meridian — welcome. You joined at a genuinely exciting time, and we mean that. Take a moment over the coming weeks to introduce yourself to the people around you, grab coffee, and get curious about what everyone is working on. That cross-team curiosity is one of the things that makes this company move.

---

**SUSTAINABILITY SPOTLIGHT: The Green Fab Initiative Gains Momentum**

Earlier this year, Meridian committed to reducing water consumption across both fabrication facilities by 15 percent before the end of 2027. We are happy to report that our Dresden fab is already ahead of schedule, having implemented a recirculating cooling system upgrade that is projected to reduce annual water usage at that site by roughly 11 percent on its own. The Taipei team has begun a parallel assessment and expects to share preliminary results in Q1 2026. This work is led by our Facilities Engineering and Environmental Compliance groups, and it reflects something CEO David Hargrove has said more than once: responsible manufacturing is not a constraint on how we build — it is part of how we define quality. More details on the broader sustainability roadmap will be shared company-wide in January.

---

**IMPORTANT NEWS: The Austin Campus Coffee Situation Has Been Resolved**

We are legally required to inform you that the Building 3 espresso machine has been replaced. The new unit is operational, calibrated, and has already received what we can only describe as an emotional reception from the third-floor firmware team. Additional updated machines are now live in Buildings 1 and 5 as well, part of a broader campus amenities refresh that Facilities has been quietly executing all quarter. Feedback forms are available — though based on what we have heard in the hallways, the reviews are already in.

---

**EMPLOYEE SPOTLIGHT: Katrin Meissner, Senior Process Integration Engineer, Dresden**

Every quarter we take a moment to highlight one of the thousands of people doing remarkable work across Meridian. This quarter, we are proud to spotlight Katrin Meissner, who joined our Dresden fab three years ago and has since become a key contributor to process yield improvements on our Axis product line. Colleagues describe Katrin as someone who asks the question everyone else was afraid to ask — and then actually goes and finds the answer. Outside of work, Katrin volunteers as a mentor for a regional STEM program supporting secondary school students interested in engineering careers. When we asked her what she would tell a student considering the semiconductor industry, she said: "Tell them the problems are real, the work is hard, and there is nothing quite like it." We think that says it well. Thank you, Katrin.

---

**LOOKING AHEAD**

The new year brings a lot to be excited about. CTO Lisa Phan and CFO Raymond Okafor will be sharing broader 2026 strategic priorities with employees in January through town halls scheduled across all sites and time zones. Details will be distributed through your regional managers in early January.

Until then — enjoy your time off, rest well, and come back in January ready for what comes next.

Happy holidays from all of us at Meridian Dynamics Communications.

---

*Meridian Dynamics | Austin, TX | MRDC*
*Questions or newsletter submissions: comms@meridiandynamics.com*

title: Email Samsung Exclusivity Legal
content:
FROM: Margaret Liu, General Counsel (m.liu@meridiandynamics.com)
TO: Lisa Phan, Chief Technology Officer (l.phan@meridiandynamics.com)
CC: David Hargrove, Chief Executive Officer (d.hargrove@meridiandynamics.com)
DATE: January 20, 2026
SUBJECT: Samsung Advanced Foundry Partnership — Exclusivity Clause Reminder [CONFIDENTIAL – ATTORNEY-CLIENT PRIVILEGED]

Lisa,

I am writing to ensure alignment ahead of your upcoming roadmap review sessions for the next-generation Axis architecture. Given the discussions I understand are taking place regarding alternative foundry sourcing, I want to bring certain contractual constraints to your direct attention.

As you will recall, the Master Foundry Agreement executed with Samsung Advanced Foundry in March 2024 contains a confidential exclusivity provision under Section 7.4(b). That clause explicitly prohibits Meridian Dynamics from entering into any manufacturing or process development agreement with TSMC — or any TSMC affiliate or subsidiary — for the production of data-center inference semiconductor devices until December 31, 2028. The restriction applies specifically to products falling within the Axis product family and any successor architectures serving the same market segment as defined therein. Pilot and Lumen product lines are not subject to this restriction.

This obligation is binding and enforceable. Any engagement with TSMC for Axis-class production prior to the contractual sunset date would constitute a material breach, exposing Meridian to significant financial penalties and potential injunctive relief as detailed in Section 14.2 of that agreement. I would ask that this constraint be factored explicitly into any internal sourcing discussions, and that no exploratory conversations with TSMC on data-center inference capacity occur — formal or informal — without prior written clearance from my office.

I recognize this creates a genuine strategic difficulty if Samsung's 3nm yield performance does not improve to acceptable levels within the near-term planning window. If that scenario materializes, please loop me in early so that we can evaluate whether any contractual remedies, renegotiation leverage, or force majeure arguments may be available to us. I would rather engage with that analysis proactively than reactively.

One additional matter requires your attention regarding external communications. The partnership with Samsung has been characterized publicly — including in investor materials, press releases, and analyst briefings — as a "non-exclusive collaboration." That characterization is intentional and was agreed upon with Samsung as a condition of the partnership. It is important that this public positioning be maintained consistently across all external-facing statements, including any comments made in technical forums, conference presentations, or media interactions by members of your organization. Questions about foundry strategy should continue to be handled through the standard investor relations protocol.

Please do not hesitate to reach out if you would like to schedule time to walk through the relevant contract provisions in detail. I am also available to join any internal roadmap sessions where sourcing decisions are on the agenda.

Regards,

Margaret Liu
General Counsel
Meridian Dynamics, Inc.
4400 Domain Boulevard, Suite 900
Austin, Texas 78758
m.liu@meridiandynamics.com

CONFIDENTIAL — This communication is protected by attorney-client privilege and is intended solely for the named recipients. Do not forward or distribute without authorization.

title: Slack Samsung Exclusivity
content:
#chip-architecture

---

**Wei Zhao** [10:14 AM]
okay so Amy and I have been going back and forth on the Axis-4 tape-out timeline and we keep hitting the same wall. if we're targeting Q3 2027 for first silicon we basically need to lock a foundry by end of Q2. TSMC N2 is the obvious call for the power envelope we need at data-center scale. can we just make that happen or is the Samsung thing still a problem

---

**Amy Zhou** [10:18 AM]
it's still a problem. I already had Marcus run through the contract language again last month. the exclusivity clause is pretty explicit — Samsung gets right of first refusal AND exclusivity on all data-center inference silicon through end of 2027. we're locked until January 2028 at the earliest

---

**Wei Zhao** [10:19 AM]
ugh. so we're supposed to tape out Axis-4 on SF3 when TSMC N2 is sitting right there. that's a real competitive disadvantage. do we even know how Samsung's SF3 yield looks for something at this die size? because the last numbers I saw were not inspiring

---

**Amy Zhou** [10:22 AM]
yeah I'm not going to sugarcoat it, SF3 yield data at >600mm² is still pretty rough. their 3GAE has been okay but we're not doing 3GAE for this. the thermal density requirements alone push us to the advanced node. I'm honestly worried about tapeout risk if we go that route

---

**Lisa Phan** [10:31 AM]
hey sorry jumping in late, was in the board prep call. Wei, I hear you, I've had the same frustration for six months. but yes the Samsung clause is real and it's not something we can work around on the Axis line. data-center inference is specifically called out in section 4.2. we negotiated hard on the definition at the time and inference was the carve-out they insisted on. it is what it is until 2028

---

**Wei Zhao** [10:33 AM]
I know I know. I just hate that we're going into a cycle where Nvidia and AMD are both on TSMC N2 and we're explaining to customers why our power numbers look the way they do. that's going to be a tough conversation for the sales team

---

**Lisa Phan** [10:35 AM]
agreed. we'll need to work with product marketing on positioning. Samsung has made commitments on yield improvement by mid-2026 and we're holding them to that. I'll be on a call with their foundry team next week and this is top of my list

---

**Amy Zhou** [10:38 AM]
Lisa — actually this is something I wanted to raise while we're all here. Lumen is a different story right? training accelerators, not inference. is there any reading of the contract where Lumen-3 falls outside the exclusivity scope? because we're starting to think about Lumen-3 node selection and if there's any flexibility there it would really change our roadmap options

---

**Lisa Phan** [10:41 AM]
that's actually a really good question and honestly I'm not 100% sure. the clause says "data-center inference" pretty specifically. training *could* be a different product category. I don't want to make any assumptions here though — if we get this wrong and Samsung finds out we went to TSMC on Lumen while the agreement is active, even if we think we're technically clean, that's a relationship problem we don't want right now given everything else

---

**Amy Zhou** [10:43 AM]
totally fair. I'm not saying we do anything without legal sign-off, just want to know if it's worth exploring

---

**Lisa Phan** [10:44 AM]
yes, absolutely worth exploring. I'll loop in Rachel's team this week and get a proper read on whether Lumen falls outside the scope. if there's a legitimate path to TSMC for training silicon we should know that ASAP so it can actually inform the planning. give me a few days

---

**Wei Zhao** [10:46 AM]
please and thank you. even getting Lumen-3 on N2 would help us a lot. at least we'd have one flagship product showing what the architecture can actually do at the right node

---

**Amy Zhou** [10:47 AM]
👍 appreciate you checking Lisa. Wei let's keep the Axis-4 planning moving on the SF3 assumption for now and we can revisit if the legal answer on Lumen opens anything up

---

**Wei Zhao** [10:48 AM]
yeah agreed. not happy about it but let's keep moving 😑

title: Memo Hr Layoff Implementation
content:
MERIDIAN DYNAMICS, INC.
INTERNAL MEMORANDUM — CONFIDENTIAL

TO: David Hargrove, Chief Executive Officer
Margaret Liu, General Counsel
FROM: Patricia Nolan, Vice President, Human Resources
DATE: March 20, 2026
SUBJECT: Q3 Workforce Reduction — Implementation Planning

---

PURPOSE

This memorandum outlines the implementation framework for the workforce reduction approved by the Board of Directors on February 18, 2026. The plan addresses departmental scope, severance structure, legal compliance timelines, employee communications, and outplacement support. All elements described herein are subject to final review by Legal prior to execution.

---

REDUCTION SCOPE AND RATIONALE

The Board authorized a reduction of approximately 7% of the global workforce, affecting an estimated 290 positions. As communicated in the February 18 session, this action is intended to streamline operating costs in response to continued margin compression in the enterprise sales segment and to realign headcount with revised FY2026 revenue projections.

Consistent with the Board's directive to protect core technology investment, Research and Development functions in Austin have been fully exempted from the reduction. The affected populations are distributed as follows:

Corporate Functions (Finance, Legal, HR, IT, Marketing): approximately 140 positions
Sales and Business Development: approximately 95 positions
Operations (non-fab administrative and supply chain coordination): approximately 55 positions

Final position-level selections within each department have been completed by the respective function heads in coordination with HR Business Partners. All selections have been reviewed against EEO criteria, and Margaret's team has conducted a preliminary adverse impact analysis. A follow-up legal review is scheduled for March 24.

---

WARN ACT COMPLIANCE

Because affected headcount in the Austin headquarters location exceeds 50 employees within a 30-day window, the federal Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification Act requires a minimum 60-day advance notice period. WARN notices to affected employees and applicable government agencies will be issued no later than March 27, 2026, establishing a notification-compliant separation effective date of May 26, 2026, for the domestic population.

HR will coordinate with outside counsel to confirm whether any affected employees in California or other states with enhanced WARN requirements are included in the final list. State-level notices will be filed concurrently. The Operations team has confirmed that neither the Taipei nor Dresden facilities trigger local mass-layoff notification thresholds at current reduction levels, though local HR leads will manage any applicable works council or regulatory consultation requirements under their respective jurisdictions.

---

SEVERANCE TERMS

All affected employees in good standing will receive the following separation package, subject to execution of a standard release agreement:

Base Severance: 12 weeks of base salary continuation, paid on regular payroll schedule.

Healthcare Continuation: Company-subsidized COBRA coverage for 12 weeks, with Meridian covering the full premium equivalent to active-employee rates.

Equity Treatment: Unvested equity awards will be treated per the standard termination provisions of the 2021 Equity Incentive Plan. Employees will have 90 days post-separation to exercise any vested options.

PTO Payout: All accrued and unused paid time-off balances will be paid in full at separation in accordance with applicable state law.

Employees in director-level and above roles will receive an enhanced severance review on a case-by-case basis, coordinated between HR and the CEO's office. Raymond Okafor's team has confirmed that total severance and benefits costs are captured in the Q2 restructuring charge, estimated at $14.2 million, previously disclosed in the February Board materials.

---

OUTPLACEMENT SERVICES

Meridian will provide outplacement support through our existing agreement with Challenger, Gray & Christmas. All affected employees will receive access to a minimum of eight weeks of one-on-one career coaching, resume development support, and job search resources through the Challenger platform. Director-level and above employees will receive an extended 16-week individualized program. Access codes and enrollment instructions will be included in each employee's separation packet.

HR will also host two optional group webinars covering benefits continuation, 401(k) rollover options, and LinkedIn profile guidance during the WARN notice period.

---

COMMUNICATION PLAN

Notification will occur in a single coordinated effort across all affected locations on the morning of April 1, 2026, following the close of WARN Act filing. The sequence is as follows:

April 1, 7:00 AM CT: Senior leadership briefing (EVPs and above) via secure video call.
April 1, 8:30 AM CT: Manager notifications for all managers with affected direct reports.
April 1, 9:00 AM CT – 12:00 PM CT: Individual employee notification meetings conducted by managers, with an HR Business Partner present or available by phone.
April 1, 12:30 PM CT: Company-wide message from David to all employees, acknowledging the reduction and affirming the company's strategic direction.
April 1, 2:00 PM CT: External press release and investor communication, coordinated with Investor Relations and Margaret's office.

All managers conducting notification meetings will receive a briefing guide and talking points from HR no later than March 30. A dedicated HR inbox will be established to handle employee questions during the notice period.

---

NEXT STEPS AND APPROVALS REQUIRED

I am requesting your review and approval of this implementation framework by March 25 in order to maintain the April 1 notification timeline. Specific items pending approval include the final position list (attached under separate cover, marked Confidential — Restricted), the severance agreement template reviewed by outside counsel, and the draft employee and company-wide communications.

Please direct any questions or concerns to me directly. I will schedule a working session with Margaret and Raymond for March 26 to confirm all pre-notification items are complete.

Patricia Nolan
Vice President, Human Resources
Meridian Dynamics, Inc.
pnolan@meridiandynamics.com

CONFIDENTIAL — FOR INTERNAL USE ONLY
Distribution of this document outside the named recipients requires prior authorization from the Office of General Counsel.

title: Slack Finance Q2 Forecast
content:
#finance-leadership

---

**Raymond Okafor** 9:14 AM
Morning both. Clearing my calendar for the next two hours. Need you fully focused on something before this gets to David or the board deck. Can you confirm you're available?

---

**Priya Nambiar** 9:15 AM
Here. What's going on?

---

**Tom Eckhardt** 9:15 AM
Same. On it.

---

**Raymond Okafor** 9:17 AM
Okay. Q2 revenue picture is deteriorating faster than I'd like. Based on the latest booking data and what ops gave me yesterday afternoon, we're tracking to approximately $0.79B for the quarter. Street consensus sits at $0.93B. That's roughly a 15% miss if the trajectory holds.

---

**Priya Nambiar** 9:18 AM
Jesus. What's driving it?

---

**Raymond Okafor** 9:21 AM
Taipei. The coolant system redesign they started in January has constrained usable fab capacity more than anyone projected. Engineering told me the retrofit is taking two to three weeks longer per line than the original schedule. That's hitting Axis wafer output hardest — those are our highest-ASP units and the ones with the most aggressive Q2 delivery commitments. Lumen is partially affected too. Pilot less so since Dresden is carrying most of that production. Bottom line: we physically cannot ship what we committed to ship, and the slippage isn't recoverable within the quarter.

---

**Tom Eckhardt** 9:23 AM
Are we talking pure capacity delay or are there customer cancellations layered on top?

---

**Raymond Okafor** 9:25 AM
Capacity delay is the primary driver. I don't have confirmed cancellations yet but I'd be surprised if we don't see some customers resequencing orders once word gets out that lead times are extending. That's the secondary risk and it's real. Right now the $0.79B number does not bake in cancellations — it's purely the production constraint. So there's downside to even that figure.

---

**Priya Nambiar** 9:27 AM
What do you need from us today?

---

**Raymond Okafor** 9:29 AM
I need you to start building out pre-announcement scenarios. Three cases minimum. Bear assumes the $0.79B holds with no recovery and we get some order slippage on top — call it $0.76B floor. Base is $0.79B as modeled. Bull assumes Taipei gets one line back online faster than scheduled and we claw back maybe $30-35M, so call it $0.82B. For each case I want gross margin impact, operating leverage implications, and a preliminary EPS range. I also want you to stress-test what this does to our full-year guide. We told the Street $3.6B for the year. That number is almost certainly not survivable as stated.

---

**Tom Eckhardt** 9:31 AM
Timeline on the modeling? And are we coordinating with IR yet?

---

**Raymond Okafor** 9:33 AM
Not IR yet. Keep this in this channel for now. I want the scenario models by end of day Wednesday. I'll take them to David Thursday morning. Depending on how that conversation goes, we'll loop in legal and IR before the weekend. We need to be thinking about disclosure timing carefully — if this is material, and I believe it is, we can't sit on it long.

---

**Priya Nambiar** 9:35 AM
What's your read on how the Street takes this? Axis demand has been the core of the bull thesis on MRDC for six quarters.

---

**Raymond Okafor** 9:37 AM
It'll be ugly. I'm not going to sugarcoat it. The thesis has been execution and supply reliability. A 15% miss on a supply constraint — not a demand problem, not a macro problem, an operational execution problem in our own fab — is going to raise questions about how we manage capacity planning. Analysts are going to want to know why we didn't see this sooner. I want to make sure we have a clean, honest account of the timeline before we're standing in front of a microphone. That's part of what the modeling work supports.

Get started. Talk later today if anything looks off in the numbers.

---

**Tom Eckhardt** 9:38 AM
Understood. We're on it.

**Priya Nambiar** 9:38 AM
On it.

title: Slack Zurich Leak
content:
#product-security · Private Channel · Saturday, March 14, 2026

---

**Lisa Phan** [CTO] 9:47 AM
@channel flagging an incident from yesterday's Zurich event. During the Axis customer showcase, a presenter accidentally advanced to the X7 benchmark slides — the ones that were supposed to be pulled from that deck before the event. Slides were on screen for roughly 40–60 seconds before someone caught it. Two attendees from separate customer orgs got photos on their phones. I've already looped in Margaret. Wanted everyone in this channel aware immediately.

---

**Margaret Liu** [General Counsel] 9:51 AM
Confirmed. I've reached out directly to both individuals. Both are covered under existing CDAs with their respective companies. I spoke with their legal contacts this morning and both attendees have confirmed deletion and signed supplemental acknowledgment letters. We have written confirmation in hand as of 8:15 AM CT. No evidence of any further distribution. I'll be monitoring closely through the weekend.

---

**Daniel Chen** [Head of Product Marketing] 9:54 AM
Okay. First — thank you both for moving fast on this. Second — what's our posture if this surfaces anyway? Are we treating this as contained or do we need a holding statement ready?

---

**Margaret Liu** [General Counsel] 9:57 AM
Treating as contained for now. The deletion confirmations are solid and I have no indication either individual has any motivation to leak. That said, yes, Daniel — I'd like you to draft a short holding statement that we keep in a drawer. Nothing proactive. Purely reactive if something surfaces in press or analyst channels. Keep it vague on specifics, focus on our standard IP protection language.

---

**Lisa Phan** [CTO] 10:02 AM
Agreed on the holding statement. Daniel, coordinate with IR on that — Raymond should probably be in the loop given potential market sensitivity. The X7 specs on those slides would move the needle if they got out before the official announcement window in Q3.

---

**Daniel Chen** [Head of Product Marketing] 10:05 AM
On it. I'll ping Raymond separately and keep it tight. Lisa — do we know how the slides ended up in that deck in the first place? Was this a version control issue or did someone pull the wrong file?

---

**Lisa Phan** [CTO] 10:09 AM
Still investigating. Preliminary read is that the presenter pulled a deck from the shared drive and the X7 section hadn't been removed from the master file. The sanitized version was in a separate subfolder that wasn't clearly labeled. That's a process failure on our end, full stop. I'm not pointing fingers at the presenter — this is a systems problem.

---

**Lisa Phan** [CTO] 10:11 AM
Which brings me to the action item I want everyone to align on: I'm calling a full review of all demo materials scheduled for the next 90 days. Every deck, every live demo environment, every prototype unit on a travel manifest. Nothing goes in front of a customer or press audience without a second sign-off from the product owner AND someone from this channel. I want that process documented by EOD Tuesday.

---

**Margaret Liu** [General Counsel] 10:14 AM
Fully supportive. I'd also recommend we add a brief confidentiality reminder to the pre-event briefing checklist for presenters — something they physically acknowledge before going on site. I can draft language this weekend.

---

**Daniel Chen** [Head of Product Marketing] 10:17 AM
That works. I'll block time Monday morning to audit the Q2 event decks. Lisa, should this review loop in the Taipei and Dresden teams as well or just Austin-originated materials?

---

**Lisa Phan** [CTO] 10:19 AM
All sites. Taipei has the Lumen demo unit scheduled for the Singapore event in April — I want that reviewed under the same protocol. Let's regroup Monday at 2 PM CT. I'll send a calendar hold now.
